stem: A 75-year-old patient has a history of squamous cell carcinoma of the lung that was resected some years ago. He presents with pedal edema. Urinary dipstick showed protein 2+. What is the most likely diagnosis?
gpt5_rationale: Membranous nephropathy is classically associated with solid tumors, including lung carcinoma, and presents with nephrotic features like pedal edema. Minimal change disease is more linked to Hodgkin lymphoma, while MPGN and IgA nephropathy are not typically associated with solid tumors or nephrotic edema in this context.

stem:
You are asked to see a 10-year-old male emergently. He is having
difficulty breathing and swallowing. He has a fever of 38.5 C and is sitting up and leaning slightly forward on the bed when you arrive. You note that there is swelling in the submental and submandibularregions with protrusion of his tongue. You are concerned about a severe infection in the floor of the mouth. Which of the following is true of this condition?
option_A: It is an uncommon presentation of mumps of the submandibular gland.
option_B: Airway protection may necessitate a tracheostomy
option_C: . It is most commonly caused by Staphylococcus aureus.
option_D: It usually arises in children as a complication of lymphoma.
gpt5_rationale: The presentation is classic for Ludwig’s angina, a rapidly progressive cellulitis of the submandibular space with high risk of airway compromise. Airway management may require tracheostomy if intubation is not feasible. It is typically polymicrobial from odontogenic sources, not mumps, Staph aureus, or lymphoma-related.

stem: One of the popular aetiological theories of Schizophrenia is the Neurochemical abnormality hypothesis. According to this hypothesis which of the following may contribute to the development of psychosis ;
gpt5_rationale: Schizophrenia is linked to mesolimbic dopamine hyperactivity and NMDA receptor (glutamate) hypofunction; the latter can produce psychosis and is part of the neurochemical hypothesis. The other options suggest the opposite direction of known associations (GABA tends to be reduced, serotonin hyperactivity is implicated, and dopamine hypoactivity relates more to negative symptoms).

stem: A 36-hour old infant presents with repeated blinking and lip smacking. There was no fever or jaundice. The serum calcium measures 1.2 mmol/l. What of the following is not a likely aetiological factor?
gpt5_rationale: This presentation suggests hypocalcemic neonatal seizures (serum calcium 1.2 mmol/L is low). Early neonatal hypocalcemia is commonly associated with perinatal asphyxia and maternal diabetes; sepsis can also cause hypocalcemia. Hypoglycaemia is a separate cause of neonatal seizures but is not an etiological factor for hypocalcemia.

stem: IGF-I is a potent growth & differentiation factor. Peripheral tissue IGF-I exerts local paracrine actions that appear to be both dependent and independent of GH. The major source of circulating IGF-I is from?
gpt5_rationale: The liver, under GH stimulation, is the principal source of circulating IGF-I, whereas other tissues mainly produce IGF-I for local paracrine/autocrine actions.

stem: A nineteen-year-old-girl is admitted with pelvic pain, a positive pregnancy test and a 3 cm viable ectopic pregnancy seen in the right fallopian tube on ultrasound. She has already had a left salpingectomy for a previous ectopic. The best management would be:
gpt5_rationale: She is hemodynamically stable with an unruptured 3 cm viable tubal ectopic and has already lost the contralateral tube, so fertility-sparing management is preferred. Methotrexate is appropriate; expectant management is unsafe, laparotomy is unnecessary, and salpingectomy would remove her remaining tube.

stem:
A 32-year-old man is referred to the renal clinic by his GP after a second episode of gross hematuria. Past history of note includes coeliac disease. On both occasions the hematuria appears to have been closely associated with an upper respiratory tract infection. Blood pressure is 125/80 mmHg. Light microscopy of a renal biopsy specimen reveals diffuse mesangial proliferation and extracellular matrix expansion. Ig A deposits are seen on
immunofluorescence. Which of the following diagnoses fits best with this clinical picture?
gpt5_rationale: Synpharyngitic gross hematuria and biopsy showing mesangial proliferation with IgA deposition are classic for IgA nephropathy. Alport’s, lupus nephritis, and Goodpasture’s have different clinical and immunofluorescence patterns.

stem: A 26-year-old female comes to the emergency setting following a high-impact blunt trauma. She ishypotensive with large-volume intraperitoneal fluid on ultrasound (FAST). She has received 2 unitsof whole blood but remains hypotensive. Which of the following is true regarding a damage control treatment strategy?
option_A: Damage control resuscitation requires that the patient be  hemodynamically stable beforesurgery is undertaken.
option_B: Damage control surgery aims to perform the definitive operation that the patient requires asquickly as possible.
option_C: The patient should remain in the emergency or casualty area until she has been stabilized, asthis is the best place for resuscitation.
option_D: Damaged control surgery is always performed by an experienced  surgeon
gpt5_rationale: Damage control resuscitation/surgery is for unstable trauma patients, prioritizing rapid hemorrhage and contamination control with abbreviated procedures and early transfer to the OR, not waiting for stability or doing definitive repair. The ED is not the optimal location for ongoing hemorrhage control, and while expertise is preferable, it is not ‘always’ performed by an experienced surgeon. None of the options accurately reflect these principles.

stem: A young medical student has come to you to you with complaints of oligomenorrrhea, hirsuitism and weight gain, ultrasound reveals bulky ovaries with sub-capsular cysts. Most likely diagnosis is:
gpt5_rationale: Oligomenorrhea, hirsutism, and weight gain with ultrasound showing bulky ovaries and multiple subcapsular cysts are classic for polycystic ovarian disease (PCOS). Cushing’s can cause hirsutism/weight gain but not the characteristic ovarian ultrasound findings. Ovarian cancer and diabetes mellitus do not fit this presentation.

stem: 32-year-old man comes to the emergency department with an acutely incarcerated inguinal hernia. With sedation, the hernia is reduced and he feels better. Eighteen hours later, he complains of increasing abdominal pain and has diffuse tenderness on examination. He most likely had which of the following?
gpt5_rationale: Post-reduction worsening pain and diffuse tenderness suggests reduction en masse or a Richter’s hernia with ongoing strangulation, not any of the listed hernia types. None of the options (epigastric, Amyand’s, lumbar, diastasis recti) match this presentation.

stem:
Kwesi Simon Senyo is a 25 year old man whose presented with the father to you at the clinic. His father reports that Kwesi has been a stubborn child since childhood. He would skip school , bully other children , steal from home and sometimes insulted his teachers. Currently he has not been able to stay in school, he has been caught for stealing and scamming people on several occasions. He mostly lies to get money from the parents. He thinks mostly about himself and wouldn’t mind hurting other people to benefit himself. He lacks remorse when he does bad things and is confronted. 
What is the most likely diagnosis for Kwesi’s condition?
gpt5_rationale: He shows a pervasive pattern since childhood of deceitfulness, aggression, violation of others' rights, irresponsibility, and lack of remorse, consistent with conduct disorder progressing into antisocial personality disorder in adulthood.

stem: A 35-year-old lady presented with a breast lump, 2 cm X 2 cm, next to the nipple. There were three mobile axillary nodes. There was no skin involvement and not fixed to the pectoral muscle. Trucut biopsy of the lump showed duct carcinoma. Sentinel node biopsy was negative. There was no evidence of distant metastases. Given this information ONLY, the best option of her management is:
option_A: Do modified radical mastectomy, chemotherapy and radiotherapy
option_B: Do simple mastectomy, axillary lymph node dissection, chemotherapy and radiotherapy
option_C: Do simple mastectomy, chemotherapy, and radiotherapy.
option_D: Do modified radical mastectomy, chemotherapy, radiotherapy, tamoxifen, and Herceptin
gpt5_rationale: For a 2 cm, clinically non-fixed, sentinel node–negative breast cancer, the standard is breast-conserving surgery with whole-breast radiotherapy; axillary dissection and postmastectomy radiotherapy are not indicated. Systemic therapy (chemo, endocrine, HER2-targeted) depends on receptor/HER2 status, which is not provided. None of the options reflects appropriate breast-conserving management or tailored adjuvant therapy.

stem: A 65-year-old man has had pain in the left hip joint for a few years that has begun to subside but he then has noticed a reduction in the joint range of motion in the same hip. The most useful single thing he should do is?
option_A: Engage in sessions of physiotherapy to loosen the joint
option_C: Start a two-week trial of anti-inflammatory drugs to further reduce the pain
gpt5_rationale: Longstanding hip pain with progressive loss of range of motion is most consistent with advanced osteoarthritis. NSAIDs won't restore motion and RF testing is not relevant; physiotherapy has limited benefit in end-stage disease. Total hip replacement is the single most effective intervention to improve function and ROM.

stem: A 68-year-old man is admitted to hospital with pneumonia and hyponatraemia with a sodium of 116 mmol/l. He was treated with antibiotics and rapid infusions of 3% hypertonic soline. Despite initial improvement, he started having seizures and went into a coma the next day. What is the possible cause of his deterioration?
option_C: Syndrome of inappropriate antidiuretic hormone secretion
gpt5_rationale: Severe hyponatraemia rapidly corrected with hypertonic saline can cause osmotic demyelination, classically central pontine myelinolysis, leading to acute neurologic deterioration including seizures and coma. SIADH explains the hyponatraemia but not the sudden decline after rapid correction, and stroke or meningitis are less consistent with the timing and context.

stem: A 24 year old man who has suffered from recurrent shoulder dislocation is found to have an early diastolic murmur best heard at left third intercostal space. He is tall and thin with a long arm span. What is the likely cause of the murmur?
gpt5_rationale: An early diastolic murmur at the left 3rd intercostal space (Erb’s point) in a tall, hypermobile patient suggests aortic regurgitation due to Marfan-associated aortic root dilation. Aortic regurgitation is not among the options, and the listed choices do not match the murmur’s timing or location.

stem: A 28-year-old male was injured in a motorcycle accident in which he was not wearing a helmet. On admission to the emergency room he was in severe respiratory distress and hypotensive (blood pressure 80/40 mm. Hg), and appeared cyanotic. He was bleeding profusely from the nose and had an obviously open femur fracture with exposed bone. Breath sounds were decreased on the right side of the chest. The initial management priority should be:
option_A: Control of hemorrhage with anterior and posterior nasal packing.
option_B: Tube thoracostomy in the right hemithorax
option_C: Endotracheal intubation with in-line cervical traction.
option_D: Obtain intravenous access and begin emergency type O blood transfusions.
gpt5_rationale: Severe respiratory distress, cyanosis, hypotension, and absent breath sounds on the right indicate a tension pneumothorax with obstructive shock. Immediate chest decompression via tube thoracostomy is the priority before intubation or transfusion.

stem: A 50-year-old drug abuser presents with fever and weight loss. Exam shows hypertension, nodular skin rash, and peripheral neuropathy. ESR is 100 mm/L , and RBC casts are seen on urinalysis. What is the likely diagnosis?
gpt5_rationale: The constellation of fever, weight loss, hypertension, nodular skin rash, peripheral neuropathy, and RBC casts suggests a systemic vasculitis such as polyarteritis nodosa, which is not among the options. The listed choices do not fit this presentation; drug-induced lupus typically spares the kidneys and the others do not cause this pattern of neuropathy and nephritic urine.

stem: A 2-year old child presents with two-day history of passage of blood-streaked loose stool. There was no associated vomiting but there was history of excessive crying prior to bowel opening. The most likely aetiology is:
gpt5_rationale: Blood-streaked stool with pain/tenesmus before defecation in a toddler suggests bacillary dysentery; the most common etiologic agent in children is Shigella flexneri. Entamoeba coli is nonpathogenic, and EHEC typically lacks tenesmus. Shigella dysenteriae is less common in endemic pediatric cases.

stem: A previously healthy 8-week old infant presents with four-week history of vomiting soon after each episode of breastfeeding. Abdominal examination reveals a non-tender epigastric mass. Which of the following dyselectrolytaemia is typical?
gpt5_rationale: The presentation suggests hypertrophic pyloric stenosis, which classically causes hypochloremic metabolic alkalosis with hypokalaemia due to vomiting and secondary hyperaldosteronism. Hyperglycaemia and hypoglycaemia are not typical, and hypernatraemia is not characteristic.

stem:
A 29-year-old woman presents to her GP with small lumps on her vulva. 
Examination reveals warty lesions. Subsequently she is referred to the vulval clinic. The biopsy of the lesions reveals benign genital warts. The following drugs can be used in the treatment of her condition except which one of the following?
gpt5_rationale: Genital warts are treated with agents like trichloroacetic acid, podophyllin, and podophyllotoxin. Dactinomycin is a cytotoxic chemotherapeutic agent and is not used for benign genital warts.

stem: A 26-year-old woman presents for follow-up of her multiple sclerosis. She has had two separate episodes of optic neuritis and has noticed stutteringly progressive weakness in her lower extremities. She has a mild neurogenic bladder. MRI scanning reveals several plaques in the periventricular white matter and several other plaques in the brainstem. Which of the following best summarizes the role of interferon β for this disease?
option_A: It improves patients’ symptoms and has no significant side effects
option_B: It can cure one-third of patients
gpt5_rationale: Interferon β reduces relapse rates by about 30% and MRI activity, does not cure MS, has notable side effects (e.g., flu-like symptoms, liver abnormalities), and has no proven survival benefit. None of the options accurately reflect its role.

stem: A 45-year-old woman presents to the clinic on account of easy fatigue and generalized pruritus for the past 5 months. She has a number of spider naevi over her chest and hepatomegaly 2 cm below subcostal margin. Liver function tests done were abnormal. Anti-mitochondrial antibody test is positive. What is the most likely diagnosis?
gpt5_rationale: Anti-mitochondrial antibody positivity with pruritus and cholestatic features strongly suggests primary biliary cholangitis, which is not listed among the options. The provided choices do not match the hallmark AMA association.

stem: A patient with psychotic disorder is convinced that she has caused a recent earthquake because she was bored and wishing for something exciting to occur. Which of the following symptoms MOST CLOSELY describes this symptom?
gpt5_rationale: This is best described as magical thinking or a delusion of influence (believing one’s thoughts caused an external event). None of the listed options—echolalia, obsession, thought broadcasting, or nihilism—accurately describe this symptom.

stem: A 20-year old male presents in your emergency room with multiple facial lacerations after a motorvehicle collision. There is one particularly deep laceration through the tragus and 4 cm into the parotid gland. You remember to check the facial nerve function and find no motion around the eye or over the forehead. The nasal ala flares on deep breathing and the corner of the mouth appears normal on smiling. What area of the facial nerve is most likely injured?
gpt5_rationale: Loss of forehead movement and eye closure implicates the temporofacial (superior) division supplying temporal and zygomatic branches, while preserved nasal ala flare and normal smile indicate the inferior division is intact.

stem: A 3-year-old male weighing 16 kg (35.3 pounds) presents with second and third-degree burns of his anterior trunk, upper back, and right arm, after pulling a large pot of boiling water off the stove onto himself. This is a 23% total body surface area burn. Fluid resuscitation is started based on the Parkland formula. When reassessing this patient 3 hours after starting fluid resuscitation, what would be an appropriate urine output?
gpt5_rationale: For children under 30 kg receiving burn resuscitation, the target urine output is 1 mL/kg/hour. The other options are either too low or excessively high for standard thermal burns without myoglobinuria.

stem: A 32-year-old woman is seen at the Endocrine Clinic on account of recurring headaches and episodes of diaphoresis. She was diagnosed of hypertension 6 months which have worsened over the last few months despite starting lisinopril 10 mg daily. Blood pressure checked is 160/94 mmHg. It is also noticed that she had a large tongue and prominent lower jaw. Her body mass index is 24 kg/m2. What is the likely cause of the hypertension?
gpt5_rationale: Macroglossia and prognathism strongly suggest acromegaly due to a GH-secreting pituitary adenoma. Acromegaly commonly causes secondary hypertension and can present with headaches and diaphoresis. The other options do not explain the distinctive craniofacial features.

stem: A 1-year-old child presents with crying continuously and drawing his legs up. The mother reports associated episodes of passing bloody mucus per rectum. A long, thin mass is palpable in the right upper quadrant of the abdomen. No peritoneal signs are present. Intussusception is suspected. Which of the following is the most appropriate next step?
option_A: Administration of IV fluids and observation
option_D: Decompressive colonoscopy with placement of a rectal tube
gpt5_rationale: Stable pediatric intussusception without peritoneal signs is best managed initially with radiologic pneumatic or hydrostatic enema reduction, which is both diagnostic and therapeutic. None of the listed options reflect this standard first-line approach.

stem: A 38-year-old male presents with hypertension and worsening renal function. An ultrasound done showed multiple cysts in both kidneys as well as cysts in the liver. A diagnosis of polycystic kidney disease is made. Which of the following is the most important association with the diagnosis?
gpt5_rationale: Autosomal dominant polycystic kidney disease is classically associated with intracranial (berry) aneurysms, which can lead to subarachnoid hemorrhage. The other options are not typical associations.

stem:
A 64-year-old, otherwise healthy male is found on physical exam to have a carotid bruit. An ultrasound shows luminal stenosis in the left 
internal carotid artery (ICA) of approximately 90%. He is asymptomatic. Which of the following is true of treatment for his condition? 
A. He
option_A: He should probably be treated with endovascular placement of a carotid stent and best medicalmanagement (daily antiplatelet therapy, SBP<130  mmHg, blood glucose well controlled, and LDL cholesterol<100).
option_B: He should probably be treated with best medical management  (daily antiplatelet therapy,SBP<130 mmHg, blood glucose  well controlled, and LDL cholesterol<100).
option_C: He should probably be reassured that treatment is unnecessary at this time.
option_D: He should probably be treated with surgical endarterectomy and best medical management(daily antiplatelet therapy, SBP<130  mmHg, blood glucose well controlled, and LDL cholesterol<100).
gpt5_rationale: In otherwise healthy, asymptomatic patients with severe (≥70–80%) ICA stenosis and low perioperative risk, carotid endarterectomy plus guideline-directed medical therapy is recommended to reduce future stroke risk. Carotid stenting is generally reserved for patients at high surgical risk, and medical therapy alone is less favored for 90% stenosis. Reassurance without treatment is inappropriate.

stem: The obstetric doctor on call is asked to review a cardiotocograph performed prior to induction of labour in a 39 weeks old multigravida. Which of the following features should help to reassure the doctor that this is a normal trace?
option_A: The baseline variability is <5 beats per minute
option_B: The baseline heart rate is 100 beats per minute.
option_C: The tocograph is picking up regular contractions.
option_D: are no significant accelerations of the fetal heart on the 30-minute recording.
gpt5_rationale: A normal CTG is reassured by baseline 110–160 bpm, variability 5–25 bpm, presence of accelerations, and no decelerations. The options describe bradycardia, reduced variability, absence of accelerations, or uterine contractions, none of which are reassuring features of fetal wellbeing.

stem:
A 48-year-old black female complains of joint pain and morning stiffness over 4 months. Examination reveals swelling of the wrists and MCPs as well as tenderness and joint effusion in both knees. The rheumatoid factor is positive, antibodies to cyclic citrullinated protein are present, and subcutaneous nodules are noted on the extensor surfaces of the forearm.
Which of the following statements is correct?
option_A: Prednisone 60 mg per day should be started
option_B: The patient has RA and should be evaluated for disease-modifying antirheumatic therapy
option_C: A nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drug should be added to aspirin
option_D: The patient’s prognosis is highly favorable
gpt5_rationale: Findings confirm rheumatoid arthritis with poor prognostic markers (RF, anti-CCP, nodules). She should be evaluated for and started on disease-modifying antirheumatic therapy. High-dose prednisone is inappropriate, NSAID addition to aspirin is not advisable, and prognosis is not highly favorable.

stem: A 52-year-old man who works as a plumber and smokes 15 cigarettes every day presents to the clinic on account of reduced exercise tolerance. There is a quiet wheeze heard on auscultation of the chest. His body mass index is 30 kg/m2. Lung function test done showed FVC of 88%, FEV 1/FVC ratio of 65% and FEV 1/FVC post bronchodilator ratio of 68%. What is the most likely diagnosis?
gpt5_rationale: Spirometry shows persistent obstructive physiology (FEV1/FVC <70% with minimal post-bronchodilator change) consistent with COPD, which is not listed. Asthma would typically show significant reversibility, while asbestosis and IPF are restrictive, and obesity hypoventilation requires hypercapnia and is not characterized by obstructive spirometry.

stem: An 11 year old child presented with sudden onset inward deviation of Right Eye, following a febrile illness treated with some injections at a private hospital. He has been complaining of neck pain and staggers when walking. What is the most likely diagnosis?
gpt5_rationale: Sudden inward deviation of one eye after a febrile illness in a child most consistent with sixth cranial nerve (abducens) palsy causing esotropia; neck pain and staggering further suggest a neurologic etiology. None of the provided options correctly identify sixth nerve palsy, and the others do not fit the presentation.

stem: A 67-year-old man presents to the outpatient clinic with a CT angiogram, which shows occlusion of the distal aorta and proximal common iliac arteries. Which of the following symptoms is this patient LEAST likely to experience as a result of this finding?
gpt5_rationale: Occlusion of the distal aorta and proximal common iliac arteries classically causes Leriche syndrome with buttock/thigh claudication and erectile dysfunction. Constipation is not a typical manifestation of aortoiliac occlusive disease.

stem:
A 29-year-old woman presents to the emergency department by ambulance with a history of a severe headache for 3 days and focal seizures. Two months prior to this, she presented to her GP with menorrhagia of 10 months' duration. She was offered LNG-IUS but she declined. She was prescribed tranexamic acid to be taken during her periods. Her medications box reveals that she has taken tranexamic acid for the whole previous month. There is no history of any viral illness recently. 
What is the likely diagnosis in her case?
gpt5_rationale: Prolonged tranexamic acid use increases the risk of venous thrombosis, including cerebral venous sinus thrombosis. CVST commonly presents with subacute severe headache and focal seizures in young women, fitting this case better than hemorrhagic or psychotic causes.

stem:
You are called to a cardiac arrest in the Emergency Department where a patient who is known to the dialysis unit has been brought by ambulance. On reviewing her drugs, you note that she is taking a statin, three antihypertensive agents, aspirin and has been receiving
erythropoietin injections. Unfortunately, resuscitation is unsuccessful.
Which of the following is the commonest cause of death in renal dialysis patients?
gpt5_rationale: In end-stage renal disease patients on dialysis, cardiovascular disease is the leading cause of mortality, often due to arrhythmias, ischemic heart disease, and heart failure. This far exceeds causes like GI bleeding, malignancy, or pulmonary embolism.

stem:
A 53-year-old diabetic who weighs 65 kg sustained an open injury on his leg when a log of wood fell on his left leg. He was managing the wound at home with local remedies. Over the subsequent week he noticed that the left leg had become warm reddened and very painful to touch. He also observed a yellowish purulent discharge from the open wound. He was rushed to the hospitals where he was found to confused and weak. His temperature on examination was 38.7 o C. His pulse and respiratory rates were 106 and 28 respectively. The BP was 74/46 mmHg which did not improve after 2 L of normal saline was administered by the medical officer on duty. A full blood count revealed a WBC of 18 x 109/L . The RBS on arrival at the emergency was 28 mmol/L . 
Use the above information to answer questions 7 to 10

7. From the above scenario, what is the most appropriate diagnosis for the patient
gpt5_rationale: An infected wound with fever, tachycardia, tachypnea, leukocytosis, confusion, and hypotension refractory to fluids indicates sepsis with organ dysfunction. Gas gangrene is not supported, and bacteraemia/septicaemia are less specific than sepsis.

stem: A 19-year-old man is brought to the physician by his parents after he called them from college, terrified that the Mafia was after him. He reports that he has eaten nothing for the past six months other than canned beans because “they are into everything—I can’t be too careful.” He is convinced that the Mafia has put cameras in his dormitory room and that they are watching his every move. He occasionally hears the voices of two men talking about him when no one is around. His roommate states that for the past 7 months the patient has been increasingly withdrawn and suspicious. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
gpt5_rationale: Findings strongly support schizophrenia (delusions, auditory hallucinations, and >6 months of functional decline). Delusional disorder lacks prominent hallucinations/impairment; schizophreniform requires 1–6 months; schizoaffective needs mood episodes; cannabis intoxication is acute. Schizophrenia is not listed.

stem: 59. A university female student started lactating even though she is not pregnant. She also noticed worsening of her vision and intermittent episodes of headache and nausea. She reported to the hospital where a Head CT scan was done which revealed a tumour in the brain. Where is the tumour likely situated
gpt5_rationale: Galactorrhea, headaches, and visual changes are classic for a pituitary prolactinoma, which can compress the optic chiasm. Thus the tumor is most likely in the pituitary.

stem: A 16-year form 2 student at Starehe Boy Centre, from Lokichogio, is admitted at Kiambu Hospital with abdominal distention with cystic liver mass with septations. Intraoperatively, a cystic mass with daughter cysts was identified. Partial cystectomy done. What is the most appropriate postoperative management of this condition?
gpt5_rationale: The cyst with daughter cysts is characteristic of hepatic hydatid disease (Echinococcus granulosus). Postoperative albendazole is the drug of choice to sterilize residual cyst elements and reduce recurrence; 28 days is a standard treatment cycle. Other listed agents are less appropriate or ineffective for hydatid cysts.

stem: A 50-year-old presents with rapidly progressive change in mental status over 3 months. Numerous myoclonic jerks accompany the dementia; the EEG shows repetitive high-voltage polyphasic discharges. What is the likely diagnosis?
gpt5_rationale: Rapidly progressive dementia with myoclonus and EEG showing periodic high-voltage polyphasic discharges is classic for Creutzfeldt-Jakob disease. Other options do not typically present with this rapid course or characteristic EEG findings.

stem: A 43-year-old, lecturer has come to you with complaints of heavy but regular menstrual bleeding with flooding and clots. There is no anatomical reason for heavy flow. The most effective remedy for reducing her menstrual flow is:
gpt5_rationale: Tranexamic acid is a first-line nonhormonal therapy that effectively reduces menstrual blood loss in ovulatory menorrhagia without structural pathology. D&C is not a treatment for heavy regular menses, misoprostol is unrelated, and DMPA may reduce bleeding but often causes irregularity and is less targeted.

stem: A 119-year-old boy complains of pain in the right lower abdominal quadrant. It was associated with anorexia. Examination revealed tenderness in the right iliac fossa. In appendicitis, where does the visceral pain often occur?
option_A: a.	In the left iliac fossa and remains there
option_B: b.	In the back and moves to the right iliac fossa
option_C: c.	In the left hypochondriac region and moves to the right iliac fossa
gpt5_rationale: Early appendicitis causes visceral pain referred to the periumbilical region due to midgut innervation, before localizing to the right iliac fossa as parietal peritoneum becomes involved.

stem: You are a medical intern. The nurse informs you that an 80-year-old man who was admitted for cellulitis seems depressed and mentions passive suicidal thoughts. When you assess him, he sees a ghost in the ward and expresses paranoid ideation against the nursing staff. Which of the following assessments is MOST IMPORTANT to establish the diagnosis?
gpt5_rationale: An elderly inpatient with acute visual hallucinations and paranoia in the setting of infection is most suggestive of delirium, for which inattention and immediate memory deficits are key. Assessing registration and short‑term recall best approximates evaluating the attentional/working memory impairment central to delirium. The other options are less diagnostic for delirium.

stem: A 50-year-old woman presents to the hospital with a two-week history of malaise , anterior neck pain and has been experiencing symptoms of what feels like a severe flu-like illness. Her blood pressure is 130/78 mmHg, and pulse is 118 bpm and regular. She has moist palms and fine tremors of both hands. There is a slightly enlarged tender thyroid gland. Investigations reveal very low TSH and high thyroxine. What is the most likely diagnosis?
gpt5_rationale: Painful tender thyroid following a viral-like illness with transient thyrotoxicosis (low TSH, high T4) is classic for subacute (de Quervain) thyroiditis. Graves’ disease and toxic nodular causes are typically non-tender and lack the preceding flu-like prodrome.

stem: A 72-year-old male comes to the office with intermittent symptoms of dyspnea on exertion, palpitations, and cough occasionally productive of blood. On cardiac auscultation, a low-pitched diastolic rumbling murmur is faintly heard toward the apex. The origin of the patient’s problem probably relates to which of the following?
option_C: A silent MI within the past year
gpt5_rationale: A low-pitched diastolic rumble at the apex with dyspnea and hemoptysis suggests mitral stenosis, which is most commonly a sequela of rheumatic fever. Hypertension or MI typically cause mitral regurgitation or other findings, and congenital mitral stenosis is rare in the elderly.

stem: 45)	An 18-month-old child has been brought to your clinic for evaluation. He and his mother are in town visiting his grandmother. His mother tells you that she found him playing with an open bottle of his grandmother's medication. On physical examination, he is sleepy but arousable, pale, mildly diaphoretic, and afebrile. His respiratory rate is 20 breaths/min, heart rate is 60 beats/min, and blood pressure is 65/40 mmHg. His lungs are clear, there are no murmurs, and his pulses are weak. Of the following, the MOST likely cause for this patient's presentation is ingestion of
gpt5_rationale: Somnolence with marked bradycardia and hypotension in a toddler most strongly suggests clonidine, beta-blocker, or calcium channel blocker ingestion. Tricyclics and pseudoephedrine typically cause tachycardia, and ACE inhibitors cause hypotension without bradycardia. None of the given options match well.

stem: ECG changes of hypokalemia are due to delayed ventricular repolarization and do not correlate well with the plasma K+ concentration. Which of the following is false regarding ECG changes of hypokalemia?
gpt5_rationale: All listed ECG changes—prolonged QU interval, T wave inversion, prominent U wave, and ST-segment depression—are recognized features of hypokalemia. Therefore none of the options is false.

stem: You are requested to give an educational talk to parents of teens regarding the prevention of cervical cancer. According to the currently available data, when should HPV vaccination be performed?
option_B: HPV vaccination should be performed at any age
option_C: According to the currently available data, HPV vaccination should not be performed at the present time
option_D: Young girls and boys should be vaccinated against HPV after their first intercourse
gpt5_rationale: HPV vaccination is recommended for girls and boys before sexual debut (ideally ages 11–12, starting at 9), with catch-up through age 26 and shared decision-making up to 45. None of the options correctly reflect this timing; D incorrectly states after first intercourse, and A–C are also incorrect.

stem: Radiologic investigations are important in the assessment of interstitial lung diseases (ILD). Which of the following imaging findings is indicative of a poor prognosis in a case of ILD?
gpt5_rationale: A bibasilar reticular pattern suggests established fibrosis (e.g., UIP/IPF), which is associated with worse prognosis. Ground-glass opacities more often reflect potentially reversible inflammation, and nodular or mixed alveolar filling patterns are not classic markers of poor prognosis in ILD.

stem: Following delivery, a woman is found to have a third-degree tear and a trainee wishes to do the repair under supervision. Which two suture materials have equivalent efficacy when repairing the external anal sphincter?
gpt5_rationale: For external anal sphincter repair, polydioxanone (PDS) and polyglactin (Vicryl) have equivalent efficacy per guidelines; none of the options pairs these two. Catgut and non-absorbable materials like nylon/Prolene are not recommended for EAS repair.

stem: An 18-year-old female returns to the hospital 72 hours after being prescribed amoxicillin for pharyngitis. She is worried that her symptoms are getting worse. She has developed a skin rash and has suffered more fevers over the past 24 hours. Her blood pressure is 141/84 mmHg and her heart rate is 88 bpm and regular. Full blood count shows marked eosinophilia and kidney function test reveals elevated creatinine. Urine test shows protein 1+ and blood 1+. What is the most likely diagnosis?
gpt5_rationale: Fever, rash, marked eosinophilia, and acute kidney injury shortly after starting amoxicillin are classic for drug-induced acute interstitial nephritis. The timing and eosinophilia make IgA nephropathy, Goodpasture’s, and post-streptococcal GN unlikely.

stem: A 38-year-old man presents with mild fever and malaise. He has generalized lymphadenopathy and a maculopapular rash over the trunk and limbs with involvement of the palms and soles. He recalls that he had a penile ulcer about 3 months earlier which resolved without treatment within 2 weeks. Which of these is NOT TRUE of his condition?
option_A: These clinical manifestations will resolve without treatment
option_B: The antibody tests used for diagnosis persist after effective treatment and cannot be used to document cure.
option_C: Neurological complications do not occur at this stage of his illness.
option_D: He should be tested for HIV
gpt5_rationale: This presentation is consistent with secondary syphilis. Non-treponemal test titers (e.g., RPR/VDRL) decline after treatment and are used to monitor cure, so saying antibody tests persist and cannot document cure is not true. The rash can resolve without treatment, and HIV testing is indicated.

stem:
In obstetric haemorrhage
I. colloids are preferred to crystalloids in fluid replacement
II. Anti-D prophylaxis is required when an Rh Negative woman receives Rh Positive FFP
III Whole blood is superior to blood components in preventing acute kidney Injury
gpt5_rationale: Crystalloids, not colloids, are preferred for initial fluid resuscitation in obstetric haemorrhage; anti-D is not required for Rh-positive FFP since Rh antigen is on red cells, not plasma; and there is no evidence that whole blood is superior to components in preventing AKI. Therefore none of the statements is correct.

stem: A 42-year-old woman had a modified radical mastectomy on account of a breast carcinoma. During her recovery in the ICU that, the surgical drain that had been left in-situ yielded about 1 L of frank blood 8 hours after the surgery. The patient was taken back to theatre for secure haemostasis. What type of complication is the patient experiencing
option_A: e.	Haemorrhage secondary to surgical site infection
gpt5_rationale: An 8-hour postoperative bleed is classically a reactionary hemorrhage due to slipping of a ligature or rise in blood pressure; this option is not provided. The listed choices (primary, secondary due to infection, or tertiary) do not accurately describe this timing and mechanism.

stem: A 39-year-old African Caribbean woman presents to her GP with menorrhagia of 10 months' duration. She does not give any history of intermenstrual or postcoital bleeding. All her previous smears have been normal. Vaginal examination reveals an essentially normal pelvis. She suffers from Crohn's disease and is taking oral sulphasalazine. She is fed up and is asking for a hysterectomy. What is her first line of management for menorrhagia?
gpt5_rationale: In women with heavy menstrual bleeding and a normal pelvic exam/smears, the levonorgestrel-releasing intrauterine system is recommended as first-line therapy. Hysterectomy is reserved for refractory cases after medical options fail, and injectable progestogens are not first-line due to side effects and irregular bleeding. LNG-IUS also avoids systemic effects, which is favorable given her Crohn’s disease.

stem: 81. 38-year-old woman who underwent total thyroidectomy for multinodular goitre 6 months ago presents with persistent hoarseness. Which of the following nerves was most likely injured during her operation.
gpt5_rationale: Persistent hoarseness after thyroidectomy most commonly indicates unilateral recurrent laryngeal nerve injury causing vocal cord paralysis. Bilateral RLN injury would cause airway compromise/aphonia, and superior laryngeal or hypoglossal nerve injuries affect pitch/sensation or tongue movement rather than isolated hoarseness.

stem: A 22-year-old Caucasian para 0 woman attends maternal medicine clinic at 13 weeks' gestation following her normal dating scan. Her booking bloods are normal. She gives a history of deep venous thrombosis following a pelvic fracture after a trip over the stairs 2 years ago. Her recent thrombophilia screen is negative. Choose the most appropriate management option for this woman with regards to thrombopropylaxis.
option_A: Aspirin and TED stockings during antenatal period
gpt5_rationale: Her prior DVT was provoked by a major transient non-hormonal risk factor (trauma) with negative thrombophilia, so antepartum pharmacologic prophylaxis is not routinely indicated; however, all women with prior VTE should receive 6 weeks of postpartum LMWH. Aspirin and TEDS are inadequate for VTE prophylaxis in this context.

stem: A 74-year-old woman presents to the emergency room with sudden onset abdominal pain and vomiting. Her abdominal exam is soft withmild tenderness to deep palpation, but she is writhing on the bed in pain. The next most appropriate step in management is:
option_C: CT scan of the abdomen and pelvis
gpt5_rationale: Severe pain out of proportion to a relatively benign abdominal exam suggests acute mesenteric ischemia. The next step is urgent CT imaging (ideally CT angiography) of the abdomen and pelvis to assess mesenteric vessels. Laparotomy is reserved for peritonitis, ultrasound is insensitive, and echocardiography is not relevant.

stem: A 22-year-old Asian woman is admitted to the hospital with an incomplete miscarriage at 11 weeks' gestation. She subsequently has surgical evacuation of the uterus. The histology reveals a large mass with hydropic changes in all the placental villi with no fetal tissue. Which of the following is the most likely karyotype and parental chromosomal origin?
gpt5_rationale: Hydropic change in all villi with no fetal tissue indicates a complete hydatidiform mole, which most commonly has a 46,XX karyotype derived entirely from paternal genetic material (androgenesis). While 46,XY paternal can occur, 46,XX paternal is most likely.

stem: Management of hepatic encephalopathy involves the use of lactulose which is a non-absorbable disaccharide and osmotic laxative. The goal of this therapy to promote how many soft stools per day?
gpt5_rationale: Lactulose for hepatic encephalopathy is titrated to achieve 2–3 soft bowel movements per day to reduce ammonia absorption. Option D (3–Feb) corresponds to 2–3 stools daily.

stem: You are taking a history from Ms. Korona and she tells you that she has been arrested several times and detained for varying periods ranging from 3 days – 10 days at the Police cell. You will record this information under
gpt5_rationale: Arrests and detention are legal issues and are documented under forensic history, which covers contacts with law enforcement, charges, and incarcerations. This information is not primarily part of social history, past psychiatric history, or premorbid personality.

stem: 16)	An 8-month-old infant arrives to the emergency department (ED) with a 2-day history of diarrhea and poor fluid intake. Your quick examination reveals a lethargic child; his heart rate is 180 beats per minute, his respiratory rate is 30 breaths per minute, and his blood pressure is low for age. He has poor skin turgor, 5-second capillary refill, and cool extremities. Which of the following fluids is most appropriate management for his condition?
gpt5_rationale: The infant shows signs of hypovolemic shock from severe dehydration, requiring immediate IV isotonic crystalloid bolus; normal saline is appropriate. ORS is for mild/moderate dehydration, whole blood is for hemorrhage, and colloids are not preferred.

stem: A nineteen year-old-girl is admitted with pelvic pain, a positive pregnancy test and a 3 cm viable ectopic pregnancy seen in the right fallopian tube on ultrasound. She has already had a left saplingectomy for a previous ectopic. The best management would be:
gpt5_rationale: She has only one remaining tube; preserving fertility is paramount. A laparoscopic salpingostomy removes the ectopic while conserving the tube, appropriate for a stable, unruptured, viable ectopic. Salpingectomy would render her infertile, expectant management is inappropriate for a viable 3 cm ectopic, and laparotomy is unnecessary if stable.

stem: A 20-year-old man with an identical twin is diagnosed with major depressive disorder. His twin brother asks you to comment on the chance that he will develop a depressive disorder. Which of the following is the correct response to his twin brother?
option_A: ‘Major depressive disorder is strongly heritable. You will definitely develop it as well.’
option_B: ‘The likelihood of you developing depressive disorder is higher than your other siblings’
option_C: ‘There is only a slightly increased risk that you will become depressed as compared to the general population’
option_D: ‘The likelihood of you developing depressive disorder is the same as if you were non-identical twins.’
gpt5_rationale: Major depressive disorder shows moderate heritability; monozygotic twins have a higher concordance than dizygotic twins or non-twin siblings, but it is not deterministic. Thus, his risk is higher than his other siblings'.

stem: George plays for one of the best football teams in Ghana. He had a tough time at training, and his coach kept shouting at him. He was upset but did not react. When he got home and his cat came rubbing itself at his feet, he angrily kicked the cat. George is employing………….
gpt5_rationale: He redirected anger from a threatening source (coach) to a safer target (cat), which is the defense mechanism of displacement; here labeled as “replacement.”

stem: A 19-year-old man is seen at the Renal clinic with history of recurrent haematuria. He also complains of hearing loss. On further questioning, it is found out that his brother also suffers from kidney disease and hearing loss. Blood pressure checked is 130/78 mmHg. Abdominal examination is unremarkable. Urine dipstick done shows blood 1+ and no protein. What is the most likely cause of this patient’s haematuria?
gpt5_rationale: Recurrent hematuria with sensorineural hearing loss and a similarly affected brother strongly suggests Alport syndrome, which is not among the options. The listed choices do not match this characteristic presentation.

stem: A 28-year-old woman had an open-heart surgery for a mitral valve replacement. She developed severe haemorrhagic as a complication such that her renal function was severely impaired. The consequent hyperkalaemia was refractory to medical therapy. What is the most appropriate course of action
option_A: a.	Increase the concentration of dextrose solution and dose of insulin
option_B: b.	Increase the dose and frequency of calcium gluconate
gpt5_rationale: In severe hyperkalaemia refractory to medical therapy, especially with markedly impaired renal function, urgent haemodialysis is the definitive treatment to remove potassium. Calcium gluconate only stabilizes the myocardium and insulin/dextrose are temporizing. Revising the surgery is not indicated.

stem:
40-year-old male with a history of hepatitis B presents with jaundice, ascites and irritability. 
Asterixis was positive. A diagnosis of hepatic encephalopathy was made. Which of the following is not a known cause of asterixis?
gpt5_rationale: Asterixis is classically associated with metabolic encephalopathies such as hepatic failure, uremia, and hypercapnic respiratory failure. Inflammatory bowel disease is not a recognized direct cause of asterixis.

stem: A 65-year-old male presents with cough and difficulty breathing. He is cyanosed with finger clubbing. A chest x ray done shows bilateral lower reticulonodular opacities. What is the most likely diagnosis?
gpt5_rationale: Finger clubbing and cyanosis with bilateral lower reticulonodular opacities on chest X-ray are characteristic of interstitial lung disease, particularly pulmonary fibrosis. Bronchiectasis and chronic bronchitis have different radiographic patterns, and bronchogenic carcinoma typically presents with a focal mass rather than bilateral reticulonodular changes.

stem: 28 years old G 3 P 2 has presented with complaints of brownish vaginal discharge, passage of vesicles and excessive vomiting. Ultrasound scan shows snowstorm appearance in uterus with no fetus. The most likely diagnosis is:
gpt5_rationale: The presentation (vesicle passage, excessive vomiting) and ultrasound snowstorm appearance without a fetus strongly indicate a complete hydatidiform mole. None of the listed options correspond to molar pregnancy.

stem: A 25-year-old male presents with a severe sore throat, difficulty swallowing, and a feverof 38.8 C three days after he underwent dental surgery. There are no palpable lymph nodes, but on your exam, the posterior pharyngeal wall is red and bulging forward. A lateral soft tissue radiograph of the neck shows swelling in the retropharyngeal space. What organism is the most likely cause of this infection?
gpt5_rationale: The red, bulging posterior pharyngeal wall and retropharyngeal space swelling indicate a retropharyngeal abscess following dental surgery. Deep neck infections from dental sources are most commonly polymicrobial with predominant anaerobic oral flora.

stem: A 45-year old male presents with a painless mass of his thigh that he states has been present since he hit his thigh on a table 2 months ago. On exam, he has a 6-cm sub fascial lesion. Which biopsy type would you recommend based on this information?
option_C: This does not require biopsy at this time given the history of trauma
gpt5_rationale: A deep, >5 cm painless mass is suspicious for soft tissue sarcoma; history of trauma does not obviate biopsy. The preferred initial approach is core needle biopsy by an experienced team. Excisional biopsy is inappropriate for large deep lesions, and incisional biopsy is reserved if core is non-diagnostic.

stem: A 32-year-old male patient presents to the emergency department with a suspected left renal trauma following a road traffic accident. Which of the following statements is true regarding his management?
option_A: Most renal trauma will require operative management.
option_B: Most grade III and IV injuries, including those with devitalized parenchymalfragments and urinary extravasation,can be managed non operatively
option_C: Grade V injuries should never be managed non operatively. D.
option_D: Blunt trauma is more likely than penetrating to require operative intervention.
gpt5_rationale: In hemodynamically stable patients, most renal injuries—including many grade III–IV with urinary extravasation and devitalized fragments—are managed nonoperatively, often with adjuncts like stenting or angioembolization. Penetrating injuries more commonly require surgery, and some grade V injuries can be managed nonoperatively in selected stable cases, making A, C, and D false.

stem: A 52-year-old woman has been recently diagnosed with HSIL. A LLETZ specimen showed CIN 3 incompletely excised specimen at the endocervical margin. What is the appropriate next step in her management?
option_A: Colposcopy and Pap smear in 6 months
option_D: Pap smear and HPV - DNA testing in 6 months
gpt5_rationale: A positive endocervical margin after LLETZ for CIN 3 indicates a high risk of residual HSIL, and in a 52-year-old, immediate repeat excisional treatment is recommended to achieve clear margins. Surveillance with cytology/HPV in 6 months is an alternative for select patients but is less appropriate here.

stem:
Following a difficult hysterectomy, a 65-year-old woman has returned to the gynaecology ward. She had large amounts of morphine in the recovery area for pain relief and is also connected to a patient-controlled analgesia device. The nurses note that she is drowsy and her respiratory rate is low. The anaesthetist 
decides to perform arterial blood gas sampling. What disturbance of acid-base balance is this most likely to show?
gpt5_rationale: Opioid-induced hypoventilation causes CO2 retention leading to respiratory acidosis, which is not among the provided options.

stem: 58. A rapidly growing breast lump was found in a 42-year-old Caribbean woman with no children. The lump is hard with ill-defined edges. It is also attached to the underlying muscle. Which of the following investigations is the most important in making a diagnosis
gpt5_rationale: In a woman over 40 with a suspicious breast mass, mammography is the key initial imaging in the triple assessment and most informative among the options. Clinical exam alone is insufficient, ultrasound is adjunct (more useful in younger/cystic lesions), and tumor markers are not diagnostic.

stem: 12)	A 3-year old child with sickle cell anemia is evaluated for lassitude and pallor. Hematologic findings include: Hgb 3.0 g/dl; WBC 25 k; retic count 0.1%. Of the following, the infectious agent most likely associated with severe anemia in this child is:
gpt5_rationale: Severe anemia with marked reticulocytopenia in a child with sickle cell disease indicates an aplastic crisis. Parvovirus B19 infects erythroid precursors, causing transient aplastic crisis and profound anemia in patients with hemolytic disorders.

stem: A 38-year-old woman presents with sudden onset left sided weakness. She mentions she has been unwell for the past few days with fever and diarrhoea. She has bruising and petechiae on both upper and lower limbs. Blood tests are significant for reduced haemoglobin, severe thrombocytopenia, elevated creatinine and hyperkalaemia. Urine dipstick shows protein 1+ and blood +. What is the most likely cause of the renal impairment?
gpt5_rationale: A diarrhoeal prodrome followed by microangiopathic hemolytic anemia, severe thrombocytopenia, and acute kidney injury (elevated creatinine, hyperkalaemia, hematuria/proteinuria) is classic for HUS. Renal involvement is typically more severe in HUS than in TTP. Lupus nephritis and post-streptococcal GN do not fit the presentation.

stem: A 70-year-old non–sexually active male complains of fever and pain in his left knee. Several days previously, the patient skinned his knee while working in his garage. The knee is red, warm, and swollen. An arthocentesis is performed, which shows 200,000 leukocytes/µL and a glucose of 20 mg/dl. No crystals are noted. Which of the following is the most important next step?
option_A: Gram stain and culture of joint fluid
gpt5_rationale: Markedly elevated synovial WBC and very low glucose without crystals strongly suggest septic arthritis, likely from skin flora. The most important next step is Gram stain and culture of the joint fluid to identify the organism and guide targeted antibiotics. Urethral culture, uric acid level, and ANA are not indicated here.

stem: A seventeen-year-old girl who is not sexually active presents with left iliac fossa pain. An ultrasound scan shows a 5 cm cyst on the left ovary which is complex in nature, with solid, calcified elements and fatty deposits noted on MRI. The likely diagnosis is:
gpt5_rationale: Fat and calcified elements on imaging strongly indicate a mature cystic teratoma (dermoid cyst), which is not among the options. The listed choices do not typically present with both fat and calcifications.

stem: A 20-year old female and her partner, a 20-year-old man presents for counselling for sexual dysfunction. Prior to their relationship, neither had been sexually active. Both deny any medical problems. Which type of male/female sexual dysfunction has the lowest cure rate?
gpt5_rationale: Female orgasmic dysfunction, especially primary anorgasmia, is generally the most difficult to treat and has the lowest cure rate. Vaginismus and premature ejaculation usually respond well to behavioral and/or pharmacologic interventions, and secondary impotence often improves if psychogenic.

stem: A 35-year-old male presents with leg swelling, ascites, and flank pains. Investigations reveal elevated serum cholesterol, reduced serum albumin, and proteinuria 4+. Which of the following is the likely diagnosis in this patient?
gpt5_rationale: Heavy proteinuria (4+), hypoalbuminemia, hyperlipidemia, and edema/ascites are the classic triad of nephrotic syndrome. Dyslipidemia and liver cirrhosis do not explain the marked proteinuria, and UTI would not present with these metabolic findings.

stem: 44)	A 16-day-old infant presents with fever, irritability, poor feeding, and a bulging fontanelle. Spinal fluid demonstrates gram-positive cocci. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
gpt5_rationale: In a 16-day-old neonate, the most likely cause of meningitis with gram-positive cocci is Group B Streptococcus (Streptococcus agalactiae), which is not listed. Listeria is a gram-positive rod, and the remaining listed organisms are uncommon causes in neonates.

stem: A 45-year-old man with insulin-dependent diabetes mellitus and rheumatoid arthritis treated with methotrexate is seen in the outpatient clinic after he tried to remove some cerumen (wax) from his ear at home. He has erythema and swelling of the ear canal anddescribes severe ear pain. You are concerned about malignant otitis externa. Which bacteria is most likely to cause this presentation?
option_C: The infection is most likely to be multibacterial.
gpt5_rationale: Malignant (necrotizing) otitis externa occurs in diabetics and immunocompromised patients and is most commonly caused by Pseudomonas aeruginosa. Other organisms like Staphylococcus aureus can cause otitis externa, but the malignant form is classically due to Pseudomonas.

stem: 19. A 48-year-old woman who sustained a 5% deep dermal burn of the right lower limb. She was managing it at home with some local remedies. About 3 years later the developed a Marjolijn’s ulcer. What is the characteristic edge of this type of ulcer
gpt5_rationale: Marjolin’s ulcer is typically a squamous cell carcinoma arising in a chronic scar, and squamous cell carcinoma classically presents with raised, everted edges.

stem: A 57-year-old male with no surgical history presents with 2 days of abdominal pain, nausea, and vomiting. On exam he is distended and tympanic and is mildly tender without rebound or guarding. An erect abdominal x-ray revealed multiple dilated loops of small bowel with air-fluid levels. His last bowel movement was 1 day ago. He has not passed flatus for over a day. a. Which of the following is the best management option?
option_A: Which of the following is the best management option?
gpt5_rationale: This presentation is most consistent with uncomplicated small-bowel obstruction, best initially managed with NPO, NG decompression, IV fluids/electrolyte correction, and CT imaging. Steroids are not indicated, and immediate laparoscopy or laparotomy is reserved for signs of strangulation/peritonitis or failure of conservative therapy, so none of the options provided are appropriate.

stem: A 20-year old female presents with complaints of general abdominal pain. she reports multiple episodes of diarrhea and vomiting. On examination she is afebrile but appears moderately dehydrated. Her abdomen is moderately tender in the peri-umbilical and epigastric regions. There is no guarding or rebound tenderness. Her white cell count is normal. Which of the following statements correctly describes the next course of management?
option_A: A diagnosis of acute abdomen should be made and laparotomy performed immediately
option_B: An abdominal CT scan is required to determine the pathology
option_C: Insert a nasogastric tube to decompress the stomach
option_D: Non surgical causes of acute abdomen should be considered
gpt5_rationale: She has no peritoneal signs or leukocytosis, and the history of diarrhea and vomiting suggests a medical (non-surgical) cause such as gastroenteritis. Immediate surgery or CT is not indicated; initial management should focus on hydration and evaluating non-surgical etiologies.

stem:
35-year-old female presents to the ER with the worse headache of her life. 
She’s known to have ADPKDx. Head CT scan shows a subarachnoid hemorrhage. 
Which of the following fundoscopic findings suggest this diagnosis?
gpt5_rationale: ADPKD is associated with berry aneurysm rupture causing subarachnoid hemorrhage. SAH can produce Terson syndrome, characterized by subhyaloid (preretinal) hemorrhage on fundoscopy, unlike silver wiring, cotton wool spots, or flame hemorrhages which are features of hypertensive/diabetic retinopathy.

stem: A 49-year-old man is admitted to the hospital with a seizure. He does not have a history of seizures and he currently takes no medications. He has AIDS and is not under any care at this time. His physical examination is most notable for small, shoddy lymphadenopathy in the cervical region. A head CT shows a ring-enhancing lesion in the right temporal lobe, with edema but no mass effect. A lumbar puncture shows no white or red blood cells, and the Gram stain is negative. His serum Toxoplasma IgG is positive. Which of the following is the best course of action for this patient at this time?
option_D: Biopsy of the central nervous system (CNS) lesion
gpt5_rationale: In an AIDS patient with a ring-enhancing lesion and positive Toxoplasma IgG, the best initial step is empiric anti-toxoplasma therapy and seizure management. Steroids are reserved for significant mass effect, biopsy is for nonresponse, and radiation or malignancy workup is not first-line. None of the options reflects appropriate initial management.

stem: a patient is brought to casualty after sustaining a stab wound to the trunk. He is hemodynamically normal, and his examination is unremarkable except for the presence of a wound 2 cm above the ilium along the right posterior axillary line. This is shown in the diagram below. The best way to evaluate this injury is:
gpt5_rationale: He is hemodynamically stable with no peritonitis and the wound is in the flank/back, where FAST is unreliable for retroperitoneal injury. Laparotomy is not indicated and serial labs alone are inadequate. Observation with serial abdominal examinations is appropriate in selective non-operative management.

stem: A 30-year-old underweight a cardiotocograph (CTG) due to induction for reduced fetal movements. A drop in the fetal heart rate was observed only during the contractions and both were mirror images. This phenomenon is referred to as;
gpt5_rationale: Decelerations that are synchronous with contractions and form a mirror image of them are classic early decelerations, typically due to fetal head compression. Late decelerations occur after the contraction, and variable decelerations have variable timing.

stem: 48. A house officer stored fresh blood in the theatre blood fridge in preparation for an open prostatectomy. The patient fortunately did not need the blood transfusion due to a low blood loss. 10 days later another patient who had a modified radical mastectomy started bleeding from the surgical site for which blood transfusion was required. The house officer rushed to the theatre fridge to retrieve the stored blood. He sent to the lab to crossmatch it with the blood sample of the patient who needed the transfusion. The following are the changes seen in stored blood except.
option_A: The leucocyte become non-viable after 24hr of storage
option_B: d.The RBC become swollen and more susceptible to damage
option_C: a.The level of 2,3-diphosphoglycerate in the cells falls
option_D: c.The platelets become non-viable after 24hrs of storage
gpt5_rationale: In refrigerated stored blood, 2,3-DPG falls, RBCs become more fragile and may swell, and platelets lose viability within about 24 hours. Leukocytes, however, can remain viable beyond 24 hours, so stating they become non-viable after 24 hours is incorrect.

stem: In a patient with Dyspnea and fatigue on exertion, Cor pulmonale due to COPD is suspected. After physical examination, which of the following imaging studies is the most appropriate to evaluate left ventricle and right ventricle function in this patient?
option_B: n/aIn a patient with Dyspnea and fatigue on exertion, Cor pulmonale due to COPD is suspected. After physical examination, which of the following imaging studies is the most appropriate to evaluate left ventricle and right ventricle function in this patient? A.	CXray B.	ECG C.	Echocardiography D.	Standard CT Scan
gpt5_rationale: Transthoracic echocardiography is the best noninvasive imaging modality to assess both left and right ventricular function and estimate pulmonary pressures in suspected cor pulmonale. ECG and chest X-ray do not evaluate ventricular function, and CT is not optimal for functional assessment.

stem: 40-year-old male with a history of hypertension and diabetes who has been lost to follow-up presents with left sided hemiparesis with a right sided lower motor neuron cranial nerve 7 palsy. What is the most likely site of the lesion?
gpt5_rationale: Ipsilateral LMN facial palsy with contralateral hemiparesis is a classic crossed brainstem finding localizing to the pons (e.g., Millard-Gubler syndrome). Cortical or internal capsule lesions cause UMN facial weakness, and cervical spine lesions don’t involve cranial nerves.

stem: A 38-year-old woman is brought to the emergency room by her family. They know that she has recently been in a psychiatric hospital but do not know the diagnosis or her medications. She has been vomiting and taking little by mouth over the past few days. The woman is ataxic and confused. She has a coarse tremor that is more prominent with movement. A moderate-sized diffuse goiter is present. Which of the following treatments is most likely to be beneficial?
gpt5_rationale: The presentation (coarse tremor, ataxia, confusion, vomiting, diffuse goiter) is most consistent with lithium toxicity. The most beneficial treatments are isotonic saline and, if severe, hemodialysis; none of the listed options appropriately address this.

stem: A young man has severe abdominal pain, a temperature of 39.0 C, a heart rate of 120, and a BP of 95/60. On abdominal examination, he has involuntary guarding and no bowel sounds. Which of the following are the best next steps of resuscitation for this patient with severe sepsis?
option_A: Give 1.0L bolus of normal saline, IV ceftriaxone, and flagyl, place O2 via nasal prongs.
option_B: Give 20 ml/kg bolus of crystalloid, IV Keflex and flagyl, place a foley catheter and admit to ICU
option_C: Give blood transfusion if Hgb <11, 1.0L bolus of D5W and IV  clindamycin
option_D: Give norepinephrine to keep BP> 100, give crystalloid fluid at maintenance and IV antibiotics.
gpt5_rationale: Severe sepsis resuscitation should include 30 mL/kg crystalloid, prompt broad-spectrum IV antibiotics with anaerobic coverage, Foley for urine output, and vasopressors only if hypotension persists to target MAP ≥65. None of the options meet these standards: A gives insufficient fluids, B uses inappropriate antibiotics (Keflex), C has incorrect fluids/transfusion threshold, and D uses pressors before adequate fluids with an incorrect BP target.

stem: A sixteen year old male student presented at the ENT clinic with recurrent nasal bleeding. Anterior and posterior rhinoscopy revealed a unilateral nasal mass, which extended into the nasopharynx. What is the likely diagnosis?
gpt5_rationale: Adolescent male with recurrent epistaxis and a vascular-looking mass extending into the nasopharynx is classic for juvenile nasopharyngeal angiofibroma. Antrochoanal polyps typically cause obstruction rather than significant bleeding, nasal polyposis is usually bilateral, and inverted papilloma occurs in older adults.

stem: An elderly woman notes a tremor associated with slowness of movements and a feeling of gait instability over the past 6 months. The tremor is a slow (3 per second) resting tremor that does not interfere with fine movements. What is the likely diagnosis?
gpt5_rationale: A slow resting tremor with bradykinesia and gait instability is classic for Parkinson’s disease. Essential tremor is an action tremor, Huntington’s has chorea, and dystonia involves sustained postures rather than a resting tremor.

stem: A mother brings her 12-year old daughter to the clinic because she is concerned that most of the other girls in her daughter’s class have already started having their period. She thinks her daughter hasn’t shown any evidence of going into puberty yet. Knowing the usual first sign of the onset of puberty, you should ask the mother which of the following questions?
option_A: Has your daughter had any acne?
option_B: Has your daughter started to develop breasts?
option_C: Does your daughter have any axillary or pubic hair?
option_D: Has your daughter started her growth spurt?
gpt5_rationale: In girls, the first sign of puberty is typically thelarche (breast budding). Pubic/axillary hair, acne, and the growth spurt generally follow after breast development.

stem: A 25 years old school teacher Para 1 wants to use oral contraceptive pills for contraception. She is asking about the mode of action of oral contraceptive pills. The mechanism of action of oral contraceptive pills is:
gpt5_rationale: OCPs primarily suppress ovulation and the progestin component thickens cervical mucus, making it hostile to sperm. Inhibiting prolactin and inducing endometritis are incorrect, and endometrial atrophy is not the principal contraceptive mechanism of OCPs.

stem: The nurse in the ward informs you that a patient developed difficulties in breathing and neck swelling 4 hours after thyroidectomy. You are the medical intern on call. What is the next step in the management of this patient?
option_A: Give oxygen by mask and observe
option_B: Call the anesthetist to intubate the patient
option_C: Prepare the patient for emergency tracheostomy
option_D: Remove all the stitches and call the surgeon on duty
gpt5_rationale: This presentation is classic for a post-thyroidectomy neck hematoma causing airway compromise. The immediate life-saving step is to open the wound by removing sutures to decompress the hematoma, then urgently involve the surgical team. Oxygen alone, intubation, or preparing for tracheostomy without decompression risks delay and difficult airway.

stem: A 25-year old male underwent percutaneous drainage of a periappendiceal abscess. If interval appendectomy is to be performed, at which of the following times post-admission should it be performed?
gpt5_rationale: Interval appendectomy after drainage of an appendiceal abscess is typically scheduled about 6–8 weeks post-admission to allow inflammation to resolve; none of the provided options match this timing. While many now opt not to perform routine interval appendectomy, the question asks for timing if it is performed.

stem: A 74-year old male is referred to your hospital with a diagnosis of "appendicitis" listed in a note from the referring hospital. He has pain and tenderness in the right abdomen. He has vomited several times over the course of the past 2 days. He has a temperature of 37.5°C. His family states that he has lost some weight over the past few weeks; they don't think he's been eating as well asusual. He is tender on physical examination but without rebound or guarding. His white blood cellcount is normal. What is the next best step in your management?
option_A: Admit the patient for antibiotics and serial abdominal exams.
option_C: Proceed with ultrasound to rule out appendicitis.
option_D: Proceed to the operating room for appendectomy
gpt5_rationale: Elderly patients with right-sided abdominal pain, weight loss, normal WBC, and no peritoneal signs raise concern for cecal/right colon malignancy rather than acute appendicitis. Colonoscopy is the appropriate next step to evaluate for right colon pathology; ultrasound is low-yield in this setting, and emergent appendectomy is not indicated.

stem: A 19-year-old female is on treatment for a urinary tract infection. Her response to treatment in the past 3 days is satisfactory. Which of the following is not a common pathogen in urinary tract infections?
gpt5_rationale: E. coli and Proteus mirabilis are classic common UTI pathogens, and Enterococcus faecalis is also a recognized cause, especially in complicated or nosocomial cases. Klebsiella aerogenes (formerly Enterobacter aerogenes) is less commonly implicated compared to Klebsiella pneumoniae and is not typically listed among the common community UTI pathogens.

stem: A 54-year-old man who smokes 25 cigarettes and has type 2 diabetes reports to the clinic because of a cough and worsening glycaemic control. He has also noticed weight gain and purplish striae on his abdomen. His fasting blood sugar is 15 mmol/L and blood pressure is 155/98 mmHg. A Chest X ray done showed a left hilar mass. What is the likely cause of his symptoms?
option_A: Small cell carcinoma of the bronchus
gpt5_rationale: Features of Cushing’s syndrome (weight gain, purple striae, severe hyperglycemia, hypertension) with a central/hilar lung mass in a heavy smoker are most consistent with ectopic ACTH production from small cell carcinoma of the bronchus.

stem:
A primigravida at 24 weeks gestation has come to the antenatal clinic with a fear of childbirth and is asking for elective caesarean section as a mode of delivery. 
What would be the recommended management?
option_A: Adequate exploration of the fears with counselling by trained personnel
option_B: Discharging the patient to midwife care with advice for vaginal delivery
option_C: Enlisting the patient for elective caesarean section
option_D: Referral to another obstetrician for second opinion
gpt5_rationale: Initial management of childbirth fear (tokophobia) is to explore concerns and provide counselling/support, including referral to perinatal mental health services. Elective caesarean may be considered later only if fear persists despite appropriate support.

stem: A 21-year old woman presents with left lower quadrant pain. A 7-cm firm adnexal mass is palpated. Ultrasound confirms a complex adnexal mass with solid components that appears to contain a tooth. What percentage of these tumours are bilateral?
gpt5_rationale: The description is classic for a mature cystic teratoma (dermoid). These are bilateral in approximately 10–15% of cases, which is not represented by any of the provided options.

stem: 48)	A 6-year-old girl presents with a 1-year history of periumbilical, nonradiating abdominal pain. The pain occurs at least three times per week and lasts up to 30 minutes. There is no history of heartburn, constipation, or diarrhea. Physical examination, complete blood count, erythrocyte sedimentation rate, and urinalysis yield normal results. A Helicobacter pylori serology (immunoglobulin G antibody) is positive. Of the following, a TRUE statement regarding this patient is that
option_A: the H pylori antibody test is more sensitive in younger children than older children
option_B: the H pylori antibody test is more sensitive in younger children than older children
option_C: he positive serology should be confirmed by another diagnostic test
option_D: the prevalence of H pylori increases with higher socioeconomic status
gpt5_rationale: H. pylori IgG serology is unreliable in children and does not confirm active infection; a positive result should be confirmed with a more specific test (stool antigen, urea breath, or endoscopy). Serology is not more sensitive in younger children, and H. pylori prevalence is higher in lower, not higher, socioeconomic settings.

stem:
A 40-year-old man underwent a second kidney transplant, some 8 years after his first, but unfortunately the donor kidney never functioned. A biopsy revealed pathological features consistent with acute rejection associated with anti HLA antibodies.
Which type of Immunoglobulin is expected to account for this process?
gpt5_rationale: Anti-HLA donor-specific antibodies mediating acute antibody-mediated rejection are typically of the IgG class, especially after prior sensitization from a previous transplant. IgM is more characteristic of natural ABO antibodies, not anti-HLA in this context.

stem: A patient has recently undergone parotidectomy for a pleomorphic adenoma. At his postoperative follow-up appointment, he tells you that he is experiencing flushing and sweating at the area surrounding the surgical site when he eats. This is the result of disordered innervation of the sweat glands by:
option_A: Branches of the spinal accessory nerve (CN XI).
option_B: Branches of the trigeminal nerve (CN V).
option_C: The cutaneous nerves of the cervical plexus
option_D: Branches of the Vagus nerve ( CN X)
gpt5_rationale: This is Frey syndrome (gustatory sweating) after parotidectomy due to aberrant reinnervation of sweat glands by the auriculotemporal nerve, a branch of CN V3. Therefore, branches of the trigeminal nerve are responsible.

stem: A 64-year-old man presented with abdominal pain, vomiting, and abdominal distention. Bowel sounds are increased on auscultation, and a plain film shows marked distention of loops of bowel with a remarkably unremarkable pattern. The most likely diagnosis is which of the following?
gpt5_rationale: Increased bowel sounds and generalized distention of multiple loops with no characteristic radiographic sign favor a mechanical small bowel obstruction rather than volvulus or gastric outlet obstruction, which have more specific film appearances. Jejunal obstruction best fits these findings.

stem: 33)	The laboratory informs you that a newborn infant, now 8 days old, has an elevated thyroid stimulating hormone (TSH) nand low thyroxin (T 4) on his newborn screen. If this condition is left untreated, the infant is likely to demonstrate which of the following in the first few months of life?
gpt5_rationale: Elevated TSH with low T4 on newborn screening indicates congenital hypothyroidism. Untreated infants commonly develop prolonged neonatal jaundice, whereas hyperreflexia, hyperirritability, and diarrhea are associated with hyperthyroidism.

stem: 38)	You just evaluated a 4-year-old incompletely immunized boy who has classic varicella lesions and a history that is consistent with this diagnosis. Of the following, the MOST accurate statement is that
option_A: lesions of both varicella and smallpox follow a 7- to 10-day course from eruption to resolution
option_B: varicella lesions are concentrated on the face; smallpox lesions are concentrated over bony prominences
option_C: lesions of both varicella and smallpox frequently produce deep, pitted scars
option_D: varicella lesions are transient vesicles; smallpox lesions are persistent pustules until resolution of the illness
gpt5_rationale: Varicella lesions are superficial, transient vesicles that crust quickly, whereas smallpox lesions are deep-seated, firm pustules that persist for days and often scar. The other options misstate distribution, duration, or scarring characteristics.
