Open node biopsy is contraindicated, barium swallow and ultrasound alone are not diagnostic, and CT is for staging rather than confirming histology.

stem: You are in the emergency department and you meet one of your patients from hypertensive clinic coming in with complaints of shortness of breath at rest, lower limb swelling and a cough. You immediately think it could be heart failure and you decide to do a chest Xray. Which one of the following would be suggestive of heart failure?
gpt5_rationale: Kerley B lines are a classic, relatively specific CXR sign of interstitial pulmonary edema due to elevated pulmonary venous pressures in heart failure. While other options can occur in HF, Kerley B lines most directly indicate congestive changes.

stem: A 37-year-old factory worker develops increasing weakness in the legs; coworkers have noted episodes of transient confusion. The patient has bilateral foot drop and atrophy; mild wrist weakness is also present. His CBC shows an anemia with a hemoglobin of 9.6 g/dL ; examination of the peripheral blood smear shows basophilic stippling. Which of the following is the most likely cause of this patient’s symptoms?
gpt5_rationale: Basophilic stippling on smear with anemia, cognitive changes, and peripheral neuropathy (wrist/foot drop) in a factory worker strongly suggests lead toxicity. ALS and myasthenia gravis would not explain the anemia and stippling; overuse syndrome would not cause confusion or hematologic changes.

stem: A 52-year-old otherwise healthy man presents to the emergency room with complaints of acute-onset pain and heaviness in his right leg. He has no pulsesin the right lower extremity. His heart rate is 153 and irregular. What is the most likely cause of this presentation?
option_A: Aortic dissection that has progressed to the iliofemoral level
option_C: Thrombotic complication of atherosclerotic plaque of the femoral artery
gpt5_rationale: Acute limb ischemia with sudden pain, pulselessness, and an irregularly irregular tachycardia strongly suggests a cardioembolic event from atrial fibrillation. None of the options describes an arterial embolus; they reflect dissection, chronic PAD/diabetes, in-situ thrombosis, or chronic aortoiliac disease.

stem: A 57 year old known hypertensive who is poorly controlled presents with sudden onset headache which he describes as the worst headache of his life. You suspect he might be having a subarachnoid hemorrhage. A CT scan is unavailable to you. What is the ideal time to do a lumbar puncture to confirm your suspicion?
gpt5_rationale: Xanthochromia from RBC breakdown in SAH becomes reliably detectable in CSF after about 12 hours. Earlier LPs risk false negatives or confusion with a traumatic tap.

stem: A 78-year-old man with diabetes mellitus presents with fever, headache and altered sensorium. On physical exam his temperature is 40.2°C, heart rate is 103 beats/min, blood pressure is 84/52 mmHg. His neck is stiff and he has photophobia. His cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) examination shows 2100 cells/µL, with 100% neutrophils, glucose 0.6 mmol/L , and protein 0.78 g/dL . CSF gram stain is negative. Empirical therapy should include which of the following?
gpt5_rationale: Findings indicate acute bacterial meningitis (neutrophilic pleocytosis, low CSF glucose, high protein). Adjunctive dexamethasone should be given prior to or with the first dose of antibiotics, particularly for suspected pneumococcal meningitis. Amphotericin and doxycycline are not appropriate empiric choices here.

stem: A 78-year-old retired plumber presented to the general clinic complaining of gradually worsening difficulty breathing and dull left sided chest pain for the past 4 months. He has a 10-pack year history of smoking. Chest examination findings were reduced chest expansion, stony dull percussion note and reduced breath sounds all on the left. Chest X ray done showed a large left sided pleural effusion and pleural plaques. What is the most likely cause of the pleural effusion?
gpt5_rationale: Pleural plaques on chest X-ray point to prior asbestos exposure, and a unilateral, chronic pleural effusion with chest pain in an older man with occupational risk strongly suggests malignant pleural mesothelioma. The other options do not typically feature pleural plaques.

stem: A 50-year-old male complains of slowly progressive weakness over several months. Walking has become more difficult, as has using his hands. There are no sensory, bowel, or bladder complaints, or any problems in thinking, speech, or vision. Examination shows distal muscle weakness with muscle wasting and fasciculations. There are also upper motor neuron signs, including extensor plantar reflexes and hyperreflexia in wasted muscle groups. Which of the following laboratory tests is most likely to be abnormal in this patient?
option_A: Cerebrospinal fluid white blood cell count
gpt5_rationale: Findings of both upper and lower motor neuron involvement without sensory symptoms are classic for ALS. Electromyography is typically abnormal, showing denervation and fasciculations, while sensory studies, CSF WBC count, and CT brain are usually normal.

stem: A 70-year-old woman is diagnosed of Zollinger-Ellison syndrome and undergoes a gastrectomy. She is informed of the need to take intramuscular vitamin B 12 shots for the rest of her life. Absence of which of the following cell types is responsible for this vitamin replacement requirement?
gpt5_rationale: Lifelong B12 is required after gastrectomy due to loss of intrinsic factor from parietal (oxyntic) cells. Parietal cells are not among the options, so none of the choices are correct.

stem: A 47 year old man had a huge lipoma excised from his back. He was admitted 8 days after surgery because the wound dressing was soaked with blood. His pulse rate was 80 beats/min, with good volume. Which of the following describes the clinical condition?
gpt5_rationale: Bleeding 8 days after surgery fits secondary haemorrhage, which typically occurs 7–10 days postoperatively, often due to infection and vessel erosion. Primary occurs at the time of surgery, and reactionary within hours; normal pulse suggests no acute shock.

stem: 52. A 5-week-old girl has been vomiting intermittently for 2 weeks and has become persistent in the past 24 hours. The child seems hungry and when fed, he vomits the food. A lump is palpated in the upper abdomen. She was diagnosed with infantile pyloric stenosis. The surgical procedure of choice for this child should be
gpt5_rationale: Infantile hypertrophic pyloric stenosis is treated with Ramstedt pyloromyotomy. Heller’s is for achalasia, duodeno-duodenostomy is for duodenal atresia, and Orringer’s is an esophagectomy procedure.

stem: A 42-year-old executive complains of a bandlike tightness across the temples and neck, worse in the afternoon, usually relieved by aspirin or acetaminophen. Neurological exam is normal. What is the likely diagnosis?
gpt5_rationale: Bandlike, bilateral tightness across temples/neck, worse later in the day, with normal neuro exam and relief by simple analgesics is classic for tension-type headache. Cluster and migraine features are absent, and temporal arteritis is unlikely at age 42.

stem: A 23-year old female presents to the emergency department with pain and tenderness in the right iliac fossa that has moved there over the past 24 hours. She has no nausea or vomiting though shestates that she has no appetite. Her temperature is 36.9°C. The pain does not increase when you release pressure when palpating the right lower quadrant. Her white blood cell count is 13 x 109 cells/L and neutrophils make up 60% of the white blood cells. How would you calculate her likelihood of appendicitis based on these clinical parameters?
option_A: He has an Alvarado score of 8 hence he needs an urgent operation
option_B: The patient has a high likelihood of appendicitis.
option_C: The patient has a low likelihood of appendicitis
option_D: He has an Alvarado score of 8 hence he needs an urgent operation
gpt5_rationale: Alvarado score is 6 (migration 1, anorexia 1, RLQ tenderness 2, leukocytosis 2; no nausea/vomiting, rebound, fever, or left shift at 60% neutrophils), indicating intermediate probability. Options A/D wrongly state score 8 and urgent operation; B overstates as high; C understates as low.

stem: A 21-year old woman presents with left lower quadrant pain. A 7-cm firm adnexal mass is palpated. Ultrasound confirms a complex adnexal mass with solid components that appears to contain a tooth. What type of ovarian tumour is this?
gpt5_rationale: A tooth within an ovarian mass is characteristic of a dermoid cyst (mature cystic teratoma). Dysgerminomas, cystadenomas, and Brenner tumors do not contain teeth or similar calcified structures.

stem: 53)	A 10-month-old baby presents to hospital with respiratory distress. She has had an upper respiratory tract infection for 2 days. On examination there is intercostal recession, tachypnoea, an audible wheeze and fine crackles throughout the lung fields. The organism most likely to cause this illness is:
gpt5_rationale: A 10-month-old with tachypnea, wheeze, and diffuse crackles after a URI has classic bronchiolitis, most commonly caused by respiratory syncytial virus. Bordetella pertussis typically causes paroxysmal cough with whoop, and rhinovirus is a common cold virus but less commonly the primary cause of bronchiolitis.

stem: A 36 year-old woman presents with vaginal bleeding at 8 weeks 3 days’ gestation. She has never been pregnant before. The bright red ’spotting’ started 3 days ago with no abdominal pain. Vaginal examination revealed a closed cervix. The following is helpful in the diagnosis of this condition:
gpt5_rationale: Threatened miscarriage is bleeding with a closed cervix in early pregnancy; assessing viability can be aided by quantitative serum β-hCG trends. A urine test and full blood count are not diagnostic, and chromosomal studies are not used for initial diagnosis.

stem: A 38-year-old man who have experiencing bright red bleeding during defaecation for about 2 years reported to the OPD. He also noticed a protrusion of a mass through the anus that quickly reduces back into the anus after defaecation. What is the grade of this haemorrhoids
gpt5_rationale: Prolapse during defecation that reduces spontaneously is characteristic of Grade II hemorrhoids. Grade I does not prolapse; Grade III requires manual reduction; Grade IV is irreducible.

stem: 63)	A ten-year-old boy with Sickle Cell disease is described as having upper motor neurone signs following a stroke affecting the right side of his body. He has a right sided facial weakness. Which of the following would help you distinguish a facial weakness caused by an upper motor neurone lesion rather than a lower motor neurone lesion?
option_C: c)	Loss of taste sensation to anterior two thirds of the tongue
option_D: b)	Mouth deviating to normal side
gpt5_rationale: UMN facial weakness typically spares the forehead and preserves lacrimation, taste, and corneal reflex; mouth deviation to the normal side occurs in both UMN and LMN lesions. All listed options either indicate LMN involvement or are not distinguishing for UMN.

stem: 15. In a discussion between the anaesthetist and house officer concerning a patient being sent for thyroidectomy, the house pointed out that the patient did not require antibiotics because the surgery was a ………………………………………..
gpt5_rationale: Thyroidectomy is a clean operation, as it does not enter the respiratory, gastrointestinal, genitourinary, or infected tissues. Therefore routine antibiotic prophylaxis is typically not required.

stem: Three weeks after an upper respiratory illness, a 25-year-old male develops weakness of his arms and legs over several days. On physical exam he is tachypneic, with shallow respirations and symmetric muscle weakness in both arms and legs. There is no obvious sensory deficit, but motor reflexes cannot be elicited. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
gpt5_rationale: Post-infectious, rapidly progressive symmetric weakness with absent reflexes and respiratory muscle involvement is classic for Guillain-Barré syndrome. Myasthenia gravis and dermatomyositis typically have preserved reflexes; multiple sclerosis presents with CNS findings rather than areflexia.

stem: After nearly 48 hours in labour, a sixteen-year-old P 1 delivers a still born male baby 3.6 Kg Five days later, despite been able to void sometimes, she presents with uncontrollable loss of urine and is offensive. You notice that she has a gait while walking into your room. The vulva looked wet and speculum examination revealed a 1 cm mid vaginal fistula with pink vaginal tissue all around. The cervix was unremarkable. The best management option is
option_A: Transvaginal repair and continuous drainage of the bladder for 7-10 days
option_B: Treat intercurrent infection and plan for an interval repair
option_C: Counsel for interval repair to allow patient to psychologically adjust prior to surgery
option_D: Counsel for interval repair to allow patient to psychologically adjust prior to surgery
gpt5_rationale: This is an early postpartum obstetric vesicovaginal fistula after prolonged labor with signs of infection; immediate repair is contraindicated. The best approach is to treat infection, ensure bladder drainage, and plan an interval repair once tissues have healed.

stem: A 67-year-old woman is admitted to the Emergency Unit with fever and a cough productive of green sputum. Her pulse rate is 101 bpm, blood pressure is 80/60 mmHg and respiratory rate of 32 cpm. She is alert and orientated in space and time. Blood tests reveal leukocytosis with neutrophilia, elevated creatinine and urea level of 8.5 mmol/L . What is her CURB-65 score?
gpt5_rationale: By CURB-65: age ≥65 (1), urea >7 mmol/L (1), RR ≥30 (1), hypotension (SBP <90 or DBP ≤60) (1), no confusion (0), totaling 4. None of the provided options matches a score of 4.

stem: A 47 year old lady complains of epigastric and slight hypochondrial pain. She also feels nauseated and has been vomiting for the past 24 hours. She recalls that her symptoms started when she was eating fried plantain. The pain did not respond to over-the-counter antacids. On examination she is an obese lady. Which of the following is the most appropriate next investigation?
option_D: HI breath test for H. Pylori
gpt5_rationale: Fatty food–triggered epigastric/right hypochondrial pain in an obese woman suggests biliary colic or acute cholecystitis. Ultrasound is the first-line investigation for gallstones and gallbladder pathology. Endoscopy/H. pylori testing targets peptic disease, and abdominal X-ray is not useful here.

stem: A Para 1+1 with no living children has a D & C for a partial molar pregnancy. The couple would like to conceive as soon as possible. Which of the following is the appropriate recommendation to them?
option_D: Use Depo Provera contraceptive for one year then conceive
gpt5_rationale: After a partial molar pregnancy, reliable contraception is advised until beta-hCG has returned to normal and follow-up confirms resolution, as pregnancy would interfere with monitoring for persistent trophoblastic disease. Once beta-hCG normalizes, conception can be attempted. Other options either set arbitrary time frames or are excessively long.

stem: 23. A 24-year-old boy was involved in a head-on collision road traffic accident as an unrestrained driver in a high-speed moving car. His car collided with an oncoming car which had veered off because the driver had apparently fallen asleep behind the wheel. The boy apparently sustained a compression injury to the abdomen. He was rushed to the ER, where he was found to be very pale with cold clammy extremities and delayed capillary refill. His BP was 77/45 with a high pulse rate of 125 bpm. He had a very tender abdomen and fractured left lower ribs. A FAST revealed a free fluid in the peritoneal cavity. What is the most appropriate diagnosis
option_A: a.Haemorrhagic shock secondary to probable splenic rupture
option_B: b.Septic shock secondary to infected fractured rib
option_C: c.Spinal shock from spinal cord injury
gpt5_rationale: He is hypotensive and tachycardic with cold, clammy extremities and positive FAST showing intraperitoneal free fluid after blunt abdominal trauma and left lower rib fractures, strongly suggesting splenic injury causing hemorrhagic shock. Septic, spinal, and cardiogenic shock are not supported by the acute findings.

stem: Ulcerative colitis typically affects the rectum and extends proximally to involve the colon in continuity. Rarely it may extend about 1-2 cm into the terminal ileum. This is called backwash ileitis. Which part of the colon is involved when this is seen?
gpt5_rationale: Backwash ileitis occurs when ulcerative colitis involves the cecum/right colon, allowing inflammation to extend 1–2 cm into the terminal ileum. This corresponds to involvement of the ascending colon near the ileocecal valve.

stem: A 5-year old child who is receiving treatment for Burkitt’s Lymphoma presents with serum potassium of 5.6 mmol/l and serum calcium 6.7 mg/dl. Which of the following clinical features will match the condition?
gpt5_rationale: Hyperkalemia and hypocalcemia after chemotherapy for Burkitt’s lymphoma suggest tumor lysis syndrome, which commonly causes acute kidney injury presenting with oliguria. Bleeding gums, inability to stand, and severe pallor are not typical acute features of tumor lysis syndrome.

stem: A 70-year-old woman with chronic obstructive pulmonary disease has a T 8 vertebral compression deformity noted on a lateral chest x-ray taken for evaluation of a cough. The deformity was not present 1 year ago. The patient does not have back pains and cannot recall any injury to her back. She has not been taking corticosteroids and has no evidence of tuberculosis. Which of the following statements is consistent with this patient’s clinical data?
gpt5_rationale: An incidental, painless vertebral compression deformity in an elderly woman most strongly suggests osteoporosis, especially with corticosteroid use and TB ruled out. None of the provided options correspond to osteoporosis, and the listed conditions are inconsistent with the clinical picture.

stem: A 24-year-old presents with fever, dyspnea, and cough for 3 weeks. Chest auscultation is unremarkable. Chest radiograph shows bilateral peri-hilar interstitial infiltrates in a butterfly pattern. His HIV antibody test is positive. Which of the following is TRUE?
option_A: Cotrimoxazole is second-line therapy in patients who do not respond to macrolides
option_C: Dapsone has no role in the management
option_D: Cryptococcosis is the most likely diagnosis
gpt5_rationale: The presentation and radiograph are classic for Pneumocystis jirovecii pneumonia, for which trimethoprim-sulfamethoxazole is first-line and dapsone is an alternative/prophylactic option. Macrolides are not used for PCP, tuberculous lymphadenitis does not explain the butterfly pattern, and cryptococcosis is less likely here.

stem: 49)	You are working in a refugee camp when a mother brings in her 8-day-old boy. The mother states he started becoming irritable 2 days ago, and now any loud noise appears to cause him pain, as evidenced by muscle tightening and back arching causing his head to nearly touch his feet. Physical examination reveals only a dried packing on his umbilical cord, as is the local custom. He appears normal until he is stimulated by touch or a loud noise, and then he begins to cry, stiffens, and arches his back. The stiffness continues until he calms down. Of the following, the MOST likely diagnosis is
gpt5_rationale: This is classic neonatal tetanus with stimulus-triggered spasms and opisthotonus after umbilical stump contamination. Botulism causes hypotonia, seizures are not typically stimulus-provoked in this manner, and encephalitis/meningitis would not produce paroxysmal tetanic spasms. None of the listed options fit the presentation.

stem: A 22-year-old man is admitted to the Emergency Department with history of sudden onset palpitations. Blood pressure on arrival is 85/50 mmHg. ECG done shows atrial fibrillation with heart rate of 140 bpm and he is electrically cardioverted. Repeat ECG shows sinus rhythm but a short PR interval and delta waves. What is the most likely diagnosis?
gpt5_rationale: Short PR interval with delta waves after cardioversion indicates Wolff-Parkinson-White syndrome, which is not among the options provided. None of the listed choices match the ECG findings.

stem: A 72-year-old patient with obesity and superficial varicose veins was admitted to the surgical department with a strangulated large incisional hernia. On the next day after the operation, the patient complained of pain in the left thigh. On day 3 after the operation, during an attempt to stand up, the patient experienced inspiratory dyspnea, dull chest pain, systolic BP of 60 mmHg, and a heart rate of 130 beats per minute. Which method of treatment would be most effective in this case?
gpt5_rationale: The patient has massive pulmonary embolism with obstructive shock shortly after major surgery, making thrombolysis contraindicated. Surgical embolectomy is the most effective definitive treatment in this scenario; heparin alone is insufficient for hemodynamic instability.

stem: A 45-year-old female presents with centripetal obesity, acne, buffalo hump and hirsutism. Her blood pressure is 160/95 mmHg and random blood glucose is 21 mmol/l (3.5-6.1 mmol/l). Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
gpt5_rationale: Centripetal obesity, buffalo hump, acne, and hirsutism are classic for Cushing’s syndrome, which is not among the options. While severe hyperglycemia is present, diabetes alone does not explain the cushingoid features, and the other options are inconsistent.

stem: 40-year-old female with a history of B 12 deficiency is diagnosed of pernicious anemia. Anti-parietal cell antibodies are detected on investigation. These antibodies are directed against which of the following?
gpt5_rationale: In pernicious anemia, anti-parietal cell antibodies commonly target the gastric proton pump H+, K+ ATPase, leading to atrophic gastritis and intrinsic factor deficiency. The other receptor options are not the targets of these antibodies.

stem: A 70-year-old female, diagnosed with sigmoid colon adenocarcinoma with metastasis to the liver, is receiving her 6 th cycle of chemotherapy. Which of the following is the best blood test to indicate her response to chemotherapy?
gpt5_rationale: CEA is the standard tumor marker used to monitor treatment response and recurrence in colorectal adenocarcinoma. CA 19-9, AFP, and β-hCG are not preferred markers for colorectal cancer.

stem: You are seeing five menopausal patients in the gynaecological clinic. Each patient has one of the medical conditions listed below. All of them wish to start hormone replacement therapy (HRT) today. The patient with which one of the following medical conditions may be started HRT at the time of the visit
option_B: Chronic liver disease due to hepatitis B
gpt5_rationale: Mild essential hypertension is not a contraindication to initiating HRT, especially with transdermal preparations. Chronic liver disease, history of breast cancer, and undiagnosed genital bleeding are contraindications requiring further evaluation or avoidance of systemic HRT.

stem:
A 50-year-old woman presents with facial weakness and slurred speech, which resolve shortly after her arrival to the 
hospital. A CT angiogram shows the finding below of a 
"beaded" appearance of the internal carotid artery. Which of 
the following is TRUE of this condition?
option_A: Treatment involves surgical resection of the affected   region of the artery
option_B: It most commonly in men in the 4th and 5th decades of   life.
option_C: It only affects the carotid arteries
option_D: It only affects the carotid arteries
gpt5_rationale: The beaded internal carotid appearance suggests fibromuscular dysplasia, which predominantly affects women and commonly involves renal, carotid, and vertebral arteries. Management is typically with percutaneous transluminal angioplasty rather than surgical resection. Therefore, none of the provided options are true.

stem: A 22-year-old girl who is diabetic presents with pain in the abdomen, vomiting and shortness of breath. She also gives a 3-day history of fevers and sore throat. Which of the following is the likely cause of her symptoms?
gpt5_rationale: A young diabetic with abdominal pain, vomiting, and shortness of breath following a febrile illness is classic for diabetic ketoacidosis, often precipitated by infection. HHS is more typical in older type 2 patients, hypoglycaemia presents with neuroglycopenic symptoms, and renal failure does not fit this acute presentation.

stem:
Group of glucose transporters (GLUT) consists of five homologous trans-membrane proteins, GLUT-1, 2, 3, 4, and 5 that are encoded by distinct genes.
Which of the following GLUT is an insulin-responsive glucose transporter?
gpt5_rationale: GLUT4 is the insulin-responsive glucose transporter found primarily in skeletal muscle and adipose tissue, translocating to the cell membrane in response to insulin. GLUT1, 2, and 3 are not insulin-dependent.

stem: A 62-year-old man is rushed to the Emergency Unit following a syncopal attack. He reports passing dark stools for the past five days. Which is the following is the most important marker for distinguishing upper from lower gastrointestinal bleeding?
gpt5_rationale: Melena (dark, tarry stools) strongly indicates upper GI bleeding. The other options are not reliable markers for localizing GI bleeding; BUN elevation would help, but creatinine alone does not.

stem: A 17-year-old boy presents with facial swelling and leg swelling for 1 week. He was previously well. On examination, blood pressure 115/60 mmHg, bilateral pleural effusion and ascites. Other systems are normal. Dipstick urinalysis shows proteinuria 4+. What is the most likely diagnosis of this patient
gpt5_rationale: The presentation (generalized edema with pleural effusion/ascites, normal BP, and 4+ proteinuria) is classic for nephrotic syndrome. None of the listed options correspond to nephrotic syndrome; the nephritic options typically feature hematuria and hypertension, which are absent here.

stem: A 19-year-old woman presents to the clinic with primary amenorrhoea. She otherwise has no other complains and is well. Her blood pressure is 115/70 mmHg. She is 1.85 m in height and has normal breast development but has virtually no pubic or axillary hair. Karyotype tested is 46 XY. What is the most likely diagnosis?
gpt5_rationale: These findings (46 XY karyotype, normal breast development, scant pubic/axillary hair, primary amenorrhea) are classic for complete androgen insensitivity syndrome, which is not among the options provided.

stem: 31. A 19-year-old teenager had an emergency laparotomy with an appendicectomy on account of typhoid ileal perforation. Today is POD 2 and the NG tube drained about 1 L of bilious aspirate. His urine output was however adequate, and he does not appear to be dehydrated. What is the fluid requirement of this patient
option_A: 2L of NS; 1L of 5% Dextrose and 1L of 10% Dextrose
option_B: 1L of ORS, 1L of 5% Dextrose and 3L of Badoe’s solution
option_C: e.1L of Gastrointestinal replacement fluid and 3L of 5% Dextrose
option_D: d.1L of RL, 1L of NS, 1L of 10% Dextrose
gpt5_rationale: He needs maintenance fluids plus replacement of the 1 L bilious NG loss with gastrointestinal replacement fluid. Option C is the only choice that explicitly replaces the NG loss appropriately while providing maintenance volume.

stem: A 42 year old engineer developed redness of the glans and radial fissuring of the prepuce 2 weeks ago. A potassium hydroxide preparation of scrapings from the glans showed pseudohyphae and buds. Which one of the following Systemic Illnesses should he be screened for?
gpt5_rationale: The findings indicate candidal balanitis, which warrants screening for diabetes mellitus (and possibly HIV), neither of which is listed. The provided options do not represent the appropriate systemic condition to screen for.

stem: A 45-year-old woman has pain in her fingers on exposure to cold, arthralgias, and difficulty swallowing solid food. Of the following tests, which, if positive, would be most supportive of a definitive diagnosis?
option_D: Reproduction of symptoms and findings by immersion of hands in cold water
gpt5_rationale: Cold-induced finger pain with arthralgias and dysphagia suggests Raynaud phenomenon in the setting of systemic sclerosis/CREST. Reproduction of symptoms with cold immersion supports Raynaud’s diagnosis; the other tests are nonspecific or unrelated.

stem: A 22-year old primigravida at 14 weeks gestation presents to the antenatal clinic with a recent exposure to her 3 year old nephew who had a rubella viral infection. In which time period does maternal infection with this virus carry the greatest risk for congenital rubella syndrome in the fetus?
gpt5_rationale: Maternal rubella infection carries the highest teratogenic risk in the first trimester, particularly within the first 8–10 weeks, leading to congenital rubella syndrome. The risk declines significantly after 12–16 weeks and is minimal later in pregnancy.

stem: A 32-year-old is due to undergo a laparoscopic operation for investigation and management of an ovarian cyst detected on scan. What is the expected serious complication rate following a laparoscopy?
gpt5_rationale: For operative gynecologic laparoscopy (e.g., ovarian cyst management), serious complication rates are typically around 0.6–1.5%. An expected rate near 0.7% aligns with published estimates, matching option B (0.736%).

stem: A 7-day old infant presents with inability to move the left upper limb. Examination shows the limb is adducted at the shoulder with pronation at the elbow and flexion at the wrist joint. The likely diagnosis is:
gpt5_rationale: The described 'waiter's tip' posture—adducted shoulder, pronated forearm, and flexed wrist—is characteristic of Erb’s palsy (C5–C6 upper trunk injury) in neonates. Klumpke’s palsy causes a claw hand; phrenic nerve injury affects respiration; clavicle fracture may cause pseudoparalysis but not this specific posture.

stem: Choose from below the single best contraceptive method for a forty five-year-old woman with a BMI of 40, smoker with multiple fibroids, who is in a stable relationship, whose family is complete and who has had a peritonitis secondary to appendicitis in the past.
gpt5_rationale: Levonorgestrel IUS is highly effective, safe in smokers and obesity, and helps with fibroid‑related heavy bleeding. Combined pills are contraindicated (age >35 and smoker), POP is less effective and may worsen bleeding, and laparoscopic sterilization carries higher operative risk with BMI 40 and prior peritonitis/adhesions.

stem: A 38-year-old G 4 P 2+1 1 alive, diagnosed diabetic four years ago came for booking at 26 weeks of gestation. She has not been compliant with medications and clinic attendance. The fetal anomaly most strongly associated with overt diabetes is:
gpt5_rationale: In pregestational (overt) diabetes, congenital heart defects are the most common fetal anomalies. While caudal regression is characteristic, among the provided options cardiac defects are the best-supported association.

stem: A 66-year-old man being managed for heart failure is found unresponsive on the medical ward after suffering a cardiac arrest. The ward defibrillator shows ventricular tachycardia and one direct current shock is delivered but there is no sinus rhythm. What is the next appropriate action?
option_D: Resume cardiopulmonary resuscitation for two minutes
gpt5_rationale: For a shockable cardiac arrest (ventricular tachycardia/fibrillation), after delivering a defibrillation shock, the next step is to immediately resume CPR for two minutes before reassessing rhythm. Adrenaline is given later (typically after the third shock), and atropine is not indicated in cardiac arrest.

stem: You are seeing in your office a patient with the chief complaint of relatively sudden onset of shortness of breath and weakness, but no chest pain. ECG shows nonspecific ST-T changes. You should be particularly attuned to the possibility of painless, or silent, myocardial infarction in which of the following patients?
option_B: Unstable angina patient on multiple medications
gpt5_rationale: Silent MI is classically associated with diabetes, advanced age, and denervated hearts, and more often in women generally. None of the listed options specifically represent these high-risk groups for painless MI.

stem: A 9-year-old boy presents with fever, vomiting, upper abdominal pain and passage of dark urine of three days duration. He had taken a hypodermic injection for a foot injury in the preceding month. Physical examination reveals moderate pallor, heart rate of 80/minute, and 6 cm tender hepatomegaly. The most likely diagnosis in the patient is:
gpt5_rationale: Fever, dark (tea-colored) urine, and tender hepatomegaly following a recent injection are most consistent with acute viral hepatitis, likely hepatitis B. None of the provided options match this diagnosis; malaria would typically include splenomegaly and other severe features, while CCF, PUD, and septicaemia do not explain the combination of findings.

stem:
Which of the following is/are true about serial ultrasound scanning in twin and triplet pregnancy?
I)	In screening for TTTS in monochorionic twins, it is recommended that scans are carried out
more often if membrane folding or liquor discordance occurs
II)	 In screening for fetal growth problems, scans should be carried out every 2 weeks from 20 weeks gestation.
III)	 No evidence supports screening for fetal growth problems using umbilical artery Doppler blood flow routinely.
gpt5_rationale: Monochorionic twins with early signs such as membrane folding or liquor discordance warrant more frequent TTTS surveillance, and routine umbilical artery Doppler for growth screening is not supported. However, growth surveillance is not universally every 2 weeks from 20 weeks (e.g., dichorionic twins are typically 4-weekly), so II is false.

stem: A 16-year-old male presents with vomiting and haematuria. He had been given antibiotics for pharyngitis three weeks earlier. Blood pressure is 150/98 mmHg. Urine dipstick done showed blood 3+ and protein 1+. Elevated creatinine and urea levels are seen on renal function test. What is the most likely diagnosis?
gpt5_rationale: The presentation (teenager with hypertension, gross hematuria, mild proteinuria, and AKI occurring ~3 weeks after pharyngitis) is most consistent with post-streptococcal glomerulonephritis, which is not listed. IgA nephropathy typically causes synpharyngitic hematuria (within days), AIN is tubulointerstitial with WBCs/rash/fever, and ANCA or anti-GBM are less likely here without systemic or pulmonary features.

stem: Madam LP is seen at the antenatal at 32 weeks with a BP of 150/80 mmHg and complains of 2 hour history of blurred vision. Protein on urine dipstick was trace. Her BP on previous visits have been normal. What is your diagnosis?
option_C: C.	Chronic hypertension with superimposed preeclampsia
gpt5_rationale: At 32 weeks with new-onset hypertension and visual disturbance, the correct diagnosis is preeclampsia (a severe feature), not eclampsia, chronic hypertension, or white coat hypertension. Gestational hypertension excludes severe features, so none of the provided options are appropriate.

stem:
A 31-year-old woman presents to the Emergency Department complaining of
left-sided pleuritic chest pain that is worse on inspiration. She has no cough and no history of fever. Her blood pressure is 80/55 mmHg. CT pulmonary angiogram showed filling defects consistent with pulmonary embolism. What is the appropriate management.
gpt5_rationale: Her hypotension indicates massive pulmonary embolism, for which urgent systemic thrombolysis (e.g., alteplase) is the recommended management. None of the options list thrombolysis; heparin alone is insufficient, rivaroxaban is for stable PE, and warfarin/aspirin are inappropriate here.

stem: Painful swelling of one or more costochondral articulations, usually 2 nd or 3 rd costochondral joint presenting as anterior chest pain radiating to arms or shoulders, aggravated by sneezing, coughing, deep inspirations or twisting motions of chest is known as?
gpt5_rationale: The description matches Tietze syndrome, an inflammatory condition of the costochondral junctions (often 2nd or 3rd) with localized swelling and chest pain aggravated by movement or respiration. The other options describe unrelated syndromes.

stem: Ama is undergoing psychotherapy. She tends to be arrogant and this affects her interpersonal relationships. Her therapist explains that this is likely because she has developed a superiority complex to escape her inferiority complex. Her therapist is applying principles from……………
gpt5_rationale: The concepts of inferiority and superiority complexes and compensation are central to Alfred Adler’s Individual Psychology. The therapist’s explanation aligns with Adlerian principles.

stem:
A 40-year-old man is referred to the clinic with refractory hypertension and a slow deterioration in his creatinine. On further questioning it transpires that his brother now
requires renal replacement therapy. He has an ultrasound scan of the abdomen which is highly suggestive of polycystic kidney disease (ADPKD).
Which of the following proteins is associated with the development of the condition?
gpt5_rationale: Autosomal dominant polycystic kidney disease is caused by mutations in PKD1 or PKD2, which encode polycystin-1 and polycystin-2. Therefore, polycystin is the protein associated with ADPKD.

stem: A 46-year-old man comes to your office 6 months after an open inguinal hernia repair complaining of pain, which has never resolved after his surgery. On further questioning, he complains of radicular pain into the testicle, which worsens with sitting for longer than 10 minutes. On exam, he is hyperesthetic overlying his scar and has numbness over the ipsilateral scrotum and medial thigh. All else is normal. Injury to which nerve is the most likely cause for his pain?
gpt5_rationale: Post-herniorrhaphy pain with sensory changes over the scrotum and medial thigh is most consistent with ilioinguinal (or genital branch of genitofemoral) nerve injury, which is not among the options provided.

stem: A 45-year-old man with chronic psychotic disorder is interviewed after being admitted to a psychiatric unit. He mimics the examiner's body posture during the interview. Which of the following characterizes the patient’s symptoms?
gpt5_rationale: Imitating another’s movements or posture is echopraxia. Echolalia is repetition of words, concrete thinking is literal interpretation, and folie à deux is shared psychosis.

stem: Yesterday you admitted a 55-year-old white male to the hospital for an episode of chest pain, and you are seeking to rule out MI plus assess for any underlying coronary artery disease. The patient tends to be anxious about his health. On admission, his lungs were clear, but his heart revealed a grade 1/6 early systolic murmur at the upper left sternal border without radiation. Blood pressure readings have consistently been in the 140/90 to 150/100 range. Serial cardiac enzymes are normal, and resting ECGs have shown no change from the initial finding of left ventricular hypertrophy with secondary ST-T changes (“LVH with strain”). The thought of performing a routine Bruce protocol treadmill exercise test (stress test) comes to mind, but is rejected, primarily due to which of the following?
option_A: The increased risk associated with these high blood pressure readings
option_B: Concern that this represents the onset of unstable angina with unacceptable risk of MI with stress testing
option_C: Concern about the heart murmur, a relative contraindication to stress testing
option_D: Anticipated difficulty with the patient’s anxiety (i., he might falsely claim chest pain during the test)
gpt5_rationale: The main reason to avoid a routine treadmill ECG is that baseline LVH with strain causes uninterpretable ST-T changes; none of the options reflects this. His BP is not high enough to contraindicate testing, the low-grade murmur is benign (no severe stenosis), and anxiety is not a primary reason; unstable angina is not suggested after negative enzymes and a stable ECG.

stem: With respect to the management of gestational hypertension in a G 2 para 1 woman with a blood of 145/96 mmHg and no proteinuria at 35 weeks' gestation, which one of the following is the correct next step of management?
gpt5_rationale: At 35 weeks with sustained BP ≥140/90 and no proteinuria, gestational hypertension should be managed outpatient but with initiation of antihypertensive therapy; oral labetalol is first-line. Hospital admission is unnecessary without severe features, and BP should be checked more frequently than weekly; weekly labs are part of follow-up, not the immediate next step.

stem: 8)	A pregnant woman was found to be a carrier for severe hemophilia A (FVIII deficiency). She wants to know what is the chance having a boy with Hemophilia. Which of the following is your best answer?
option_A: 100%; unfortunately they will all be affected.
option_D: 0%; Hemophilia is dependent on the father
gpt5_rationale: Hemophilia A is X-linked recessive. A carrier mother has a 50% chance of having a son and, for any son, a 50% chance he inherits the affected X, giving a 25% overall chance of an affected boy.

stem: A 72-year-old male presents to the ER with the chief complaint of shortness of breath that awakens him at night. Physical exam findings include bilateral basilar rales, an S 3 gallop, and evidence of cardiomegaly, all major criteria for the diagnosis of congestive heart failure under the Framingham system. While patients with CHF may display a wide range of symptoms and signs, which of the following are also considered among the major criteria (i., more specific to CHF)?
option_A: Dyspnea on exertion and night cough
option_C: Tachycardia (rate 120 or greater) with new onset or increase in ectopy
option_D: Neck vein distention and positive hepatojugular reflux
gpt5_rationale: In the Framingham criteria, neck vein distention and a positive hepatojugular reflux are major criteria. Dyspnea on exertion, nocturnal cough, pleural effusion, hepatomegaly, and tachycardia ≥120 are minor criteria.

stem: Two weeks after hospital discharge for documented myocardial infarction, a 65-year-old returns to your office very concerned about low grade fever and pleuritic chest pain. There is no associated shortness of breath. Lungs are clear to auscultation and heart exam is free of significant murmurs, gallops, or rubs. ECG is unchanged from the last one in the hospital. Which of the following therapies is likely to be most effective?
gpt5_rationale: Pleuritic chest pain and low-grade fever two weeks after MI suggest post-MI pericarditis (Dressler syndrome). First-line treatment is an anti-inflammatory agent (e.g., NSAIDs). Antibiotics, anticoagulation, and increased antianginals are not indicated.

stem: 46)	A previously healthy 8-year-old boy has a 3-week history of low-grade fever of unknown source, fatigue, weight loss, myalgia, and headaches. On repeated examinations during this time, he is found to have developed a heart murmur, petechiae, and mild splenomegaly. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
gpt5_rationale: Prolonged low-grade fever with a new heart murmur, petechiae, and splenomegaly is classic for subacute infective endocarditis. Rheumatic, Kawasaki, and scarlet fever do not typically present with this combination of findings.

stem: A 33-year-old patient with known epilepsy is brought to the Casualty Department following a generalised tonic-clonic seizure (GTCS). Although the seizure has now stopped, the patient continues to be drowsy. He consumes no alcohol, is a non-smoker and has no other illnesses. Following taking a history from the accompanying family members and performing of a clinical examination, you want to undertake some blood investigations. Which of the following metabolic abnormalities is most likely to be found in this patient?
gpt5_rationale: After a generalized tonic-clonic seizure, patients commonly have transient metabolic changes including lactic acidosis and elevated serum phosphate from intense muscle activity. Hyponatraemia, hypocalcaemia, and hypoglycaemia are potential causes of seizures but are not the typical postictal findings in known epilepsy.

stem: A 3-year old child presents with fever, progressively worsening cough and difficulty with breathing. Chest Xray shows homogenous radiolucency over the right middle and lower lobe areas. Which of the following physical examination findings will be commensurate with the radiological features?
option_A: Increased vocal resonance on the right
option_C: Stony dullness to percussion on the right
option_D: Increased tactile fremitus on the right
gpt5_rationale: Homogeneous radiolucency suggests increased air (e.g., air trapping or pneumothorax), which would decrease tactile fremitus and vocal resonance and typically shift the trachea away, not toward, the affected side. Stony dullness indicates effusion. None of the options align with a radiolucent process on the right.

stem: A 50 year old male with type 2 diabetes mellitus was recently diagnosed with hypertension and started on Lisinopril. His blood pressure is however not controlled. Which of the following is the most appropriate antihypertensive to add on?
gpt5_rationale: In T2DM, an ACE inhibitor is first-line; if BP remains uncontrolled, add a thiazide (or thiazide-like) diuretic. Combining an ARB with an ACE inhibitor is not recommended, beta-blockers are not preferred as second-line in diabetes, and verapamil is less appropriate than a thiazide for add-on therapy.

stem: A 72-year-old man who had a myocardial infarction three days ago for which percutaneous coronary intervention was done experiences sudden difficulty breathing. His blood pressure is 90/65 mmHg and pulse is 102 bpm and regular. Oxygen saturation is 94% on 4 liters of oxygen. He has bibasal crackles and a pansystolic murmur radiating to the axilla is heard. What is the most likely cause of these clinical findings?
gpt5_rationale: The presentation 3 days post-MI with sudden dyspnea, hypotension, bibasal crackles, and a pansystolic murmur radiating to the axilla is classic for acute severe mitral regurgitation from papillary muscle rupture, which is not listed. VSD typically has a harsh holosystolic murmur at the left sternal border, pericarditis causes a friction rub and chest pain, and free wall rupture leads to tamponade rather than this murmur.

stem: After an initial pregnancy resulted in a spontaneous loss in the first trimester, your patient is concerned about the possibility of this recurring. Which of the following is the most appropriate answer regarding the chance of recurrence?
option_A: It depends on the sex of the prior aborted  fetus
option_C: It depends on the genetic makeup of the prior abortus
option_D: It has increased most likely to greater than 50%
gpt5_rationale: After one first‑trimester spontaneous abortion, recurrence risk is not significantly increased and remains near baseline (~15–20%). None of the options accurately reflect this counseling.

stem: 71. A 40-year model complains of pain in the right ankle when walking for moderate to long distances. She noticed some subcutaneous varicosities just below the right knee. What is the most likely diagnosis
option_A: Varicose veins of the short saphenous system
gpt5_rationale: Varicosities confined below the knee suggest involvement of the short saphenous system, which runs along the posterior calf and is typically symptomatic with aching on prolonged walking/standing. DVT and superficial thrombophlebitis would present with acute pain, tenderness, and swelling, and varicose ulcers would involve skin breakdown, which is not described.

stem: A 20 year-old female was involved in a road traffic trauma and was rushed to the casualty because of massive external bleeding. Her estimated weight was 40 kg. The patient’s estimated total blood volume (TBV) was 1,750 mls. Initially, six units of p RBCs were given followed by another 9 units given over the next 2 hours. Two doses of FFP (10 ml/kg) were also administered. What is the next appropriate step with regard to the patient’s developing dilutional coagulopathy?
option_A: Order PT, aPTT, platelet count, and fibrinogen level STAT and order more FFP (10 ml/kg), platelets (10 ml/kg), and 3 pooled units of cryoprecipitate.
option_B: Order platelets (10 ml/kg) and more FFP (10 ml/kg) x 3 doses.
option_C: Order platelets only and then PT, aPTT, fibrinogen.
option_D: Order more pRBCs (emergency release) and give platelets (10 ml/kg), then  order more FFP (10 ml/kg).
gpt5_rationale: After massive transfusion with predominantly pRBCs, dilutional coagulopathy and thrombocytopenia are expected, necessitating immediate coagulation labs and replacement of plasma, platelets, and fibrinogen. Option A appropriately includes STAT labs plus FFP, platelets, and cryoprecipitate to address these deficits.

stem: A 55-year-old man with a history of hypertension, diabetes and atherosclerosis started experiencing intermittent claudication. An angiogram shows a right common iliac artery stenosis of 90% over a short segment. What is the treatment of choice?
gpt5_rationale: For a short-segment, high-grade common iliac stenosis, the treatment of choice is endovascular angioplasty with stenting. The listed options are surgical bypasses reserved for occlusions or extensive disease, not focal stenosis.

stem: A 45-year-old asthmatic patient attends the gynaecology clinic with heavy menstrual bleeding and an ultrasound scan suggests the presence of an endometrial polyp. The patient is booked for an outpatient hysteroscopy. What analgesia should be prescribed at least 1 hour before the procedure?
gpt5_rationale: NSAIDs (ibuprofen/diclofenac) are commonly used before outpatient hysteroscopy but can precipitate bronchospasm in asthmatics. Paracetamol is a safe alternative and appropriate to give at least 1 hour pre-procedure.

stem: A 30-year-old woman comes to her GP for advice. She is a known epileptic and is currently on antiepileptic medication. She is planning to conceive in the next couple of months and would like to know the risks to her and the fetus if she gets pregnant. All of the following, are true except which one?
option_B: The risk of aspiration during seizure and sudden unexpected death (SUDEP) may be increased
option_C: The risk of congenital malformations in the foetus is increased by 2-3-fold.
option_D: Seizure frequency can be increased by 10-30% during pregnancy
gpt5_rationale: Miscarriage rates are not clearly increased in women with epilepsy compared to the general population. Congenital malformation risk is about 2–3 times higher with antiepileptic drugs, and seizure frequency increases in roughly 20–30% of pregnancies; aspiration risk during seizures may also be higher due to pregnancy-related gastric changes.

stem: A 29-year old woman comes to the physician for evaluation of a mass in the left breast noticed 2 weeks ago. During this period the mass has not increased in size and has no pain. Three months ago she hit her left chest against a door and it was painful for a day. Menses occur at regular 28 day intervals and last for 5 days. Her last menstrual period was 3 weeks ago. Physical examination shows dense breasts and 2.5 cm well defined, rubbery, mobile mass that is non tender in the upper outer quadrant of the left breast. There is no axillary adenopathy. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
gpt5_rationale: In a young woman, a well-defined, rubbery, mobile, non-tender breast mass is most consistent with a fibroadenoma. Fibrocystic changes are typically diffuse and tender, intraductal papilloma presents with nipple discharge, and phyllodes tumors tend to be larger and rapidly growing in older women.

stem: 39)	While bathing her 2-year-old son, a mother feels a mass in his abdomen. A thorough medical evaluation of the child reveals aniridia, hypospadias, horseshoe kidney, and hemihypertrophy. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis for this child?
gpt5_rationale: A 2-year-old with aniridia, genitourinary anomalies (hypospadias), and renal anomalies (horseshoe kidney) fits WAGR syndrome, which is strongly associated with Wilms tumor presenting as an abdominal mass. Neuroblastoma and hepatoblastoma do not match this constellation, and rhabdomyosarcoma is less likely.

stem: A 46-year-old woman with a history of relapsing and remitting multiple sclerosis comes to the clinic for review. She has been stable for the past 6 months and is currently treated with oral fingolimod. Neurological examination reveals poor coordination, more marked on the left than the right, although there are no other significant signs. Which of the following correctly represents the mode of action of fingolimod?
option_B: Increases the expression and concentration of anti-inflammatory agent
gpt5_rationale: Fingolimod is a sphingosine-1-phosphate receptor modulator that sequesters lymphocytes in lymph nodes, reducing their egress to the CNS; it is not cytotoxic, does not mimic myelin peptides, and does not primarily increase anti-inflammatory agents. None of the options accurately describes this mechanism.

stem: A 22-yearold man is brought to the hospital after crashing his motorcycle into a telephone pole. He is unconscious and in profound shock. He has no open wounds or obvious fractures. The cause of his shock is MOST LIKELY caused by:
gpt5_rationale: Profound shock in trauma is most commonly due to hemorrhage; intracranial injuries and basilar skull fractures do not cause hypotension, and neurogenic shock typically requires high (above T6) spinal cord injury, not lumbar. None of the options appropriately explain profound shock.

stem: Because of his involvement in a motor vehicle accident, a 23-year-old football player has a chest wall injury. The only abnormal findings on clinical and radiologic examination are a fracture of the left fifth to seventh ribs and a small hemothorax. What should treatment include?
option_A: Thoracotomy to treat a hemothorax in the left base
option_B: Insertion of a metal plate to fix the fracture
option_C: Insertion of an intercostal drain to avoid pneumothorax
option_D: Immediate surgical intervention to remove damaged ribs
gpt5_rationale: A small hemothorax from rib fractures is usually managed conservatively with analgesia and pulmonary hygiene; chest tube drainage is reserved for larger, symptomatic, or expanding collections or concurrent pneumothorax. Thoracotomy, rib plating, and rib removal are not indicated, and inserting a drain solely “to avoid pneumothorax” is not appropriate.

stem: A 54-year-old man with Schizophrenia who has been on Haloperidol for the past 25 years seems to be constantly chewing. He does not wear dentures. His tongue darts in and out of his mouth, and he occasionally smacks his lips. He also grimaces, frowns, and blinks excessively. Which of the following disorders is most likely in this patient?
gpt5_rationale: Chronic haloperidol use with oral-buccal-lingual movements (chewing, tongue darting, lip smacking) is classic for tardive dyskinesia, which is not listed. The options given (Tourette’s, akathisia, Huntington’s, Parkinson’s) do not fit this medication-induced syndrome.

stem: A 42-year-old woman on medications for pulmonary tuberculosis complains of numbness and tingling sensation in both hands and feet which developed recently. What could have been added to her medications to help prevent development of these new symptoms?
gpt5_rationale: Isoniazid causes peripheral neuropathy by inducing pyridoxine (vitamin B6) deficiency, so prophylactic pyridoxine should be added. None of the options list pyridoxine; thiamine, cobalamin, iron, and niacin are not the recommended preventive supplement for INH-induced neuropathy.

stem: In healthy adults, pleural space contains about 5 to 10 m L of pleural fluid (0.1 mg/kg body weight). At least, what quantity of pleural fluid must be present to be detected by physical examination?
gpt5_rationale: Physical examination typically requires about 300 mL or more of pleural fluid to detect effusion (e.g., dullness to percussion, decreased breath sounds). Among the given options, 350 mL is the closest appropriate threshold.

stem:
A 30-year-old male is involved in a major motor vehicle crash. During the primary survey in the emergency department, he is intubated for airway protection. He has equal breath sounds. He hasreceived one liter of intravenous crystalloid fluid. 
On repeat assessment, he has a blood pressure of 80/50 mmHg and pulse of 120 bpm. What is the best next step in management?
option_A: A. Proceed immediately to theatre for laparotomy
option_B: B. Start inotropic support and titrate to MAP > 65 mm Hg.
option_C: Immediately transfuse blood products, either whole blood or a 1:1:1 transfusion of red bloodcells, platelets, and plasma as soon as  available.
option_D: Continue ongoing crystalloid resuscitation while drawing blood samples to assess hemoglobinlevel and determine the need for blood transfusion.
gpt5_rationale: Persistent hypotension after trauma despite initial crystalloid indicates hemorrhagic shock; the next step is damage-control resuscitation with immediate balanced blood product transfusion/whole blood. Vasopressors and further crystalloids are inappropriate, and laparotomy should follow concurrent evaluation/source control once resuscitation is initiated.

stem: You are asked to see a 21-year-old woman for preconceptual care. She was diagnosed with generalised tonic-clonic epilepsy four years ago. This is poorly controlled. She is currently on sodium valproate and levetiracetam. What is the next step in her management?
gpt5_rationale: Preconception care for women with epilepsy includes starting high-dose folic acid (5 mg daily) to reduce neural tube defects, especially when taking antiepileptic drugs. EEG/MRI are not routine preconception steps, and aspirin is not indicated for epilepsy.

stem: A 9-month old presents with axillary temperature of 40.8ºC with demonstrable asexual forms of Plasmodium falciparum on blood film. Which of the complications is least likely to be present in the child?
gpt5_rationale: In children with severe falciparum malaria, hypoglycaemia and severe anaemia are common complications. Acute renal insufficiency is uncommon in pediatric cases compared to adults, making it the least likely among the options.

stem: A newborn baby delivered at term appears uniformly blue, has a heart rate of 30/minute, no grimace, a partial flexion of the limbs and irregular respiration at the fifth minute of life. The most important immediate resuscitative measure at that moment is:
gpt5_rationale: With HR 30/min and irregular respirations, the newborn is severely depressed; neonatal resuscitation guidelines prioritize immediate positive pressure ventilation. Warming is supportive but not the most urgent intervention, and saline or hydrocortisone are not indicated. Bag-mask ventilation is the key immediate step.

stem: A 20 year old unbooked primigravida was referred from a traditional birth attendants home having been in labour for 20 hours. A diagnosis of Obstructed labour was made at the centre she was referred to. The pathological retraction ring which is one of the features of obstructed labour is caused by:
option_A: Thinning of the lower uterine segment
option_B: Thinning of the upper uterine segment
option_C: Thickening of the lower uterine segment.
gpt5_rationale: In obstructed labor, the upper uterine segment contracts and retracts (thickens) while the lower uterine segment becomes overstretched and thinned. Bandl’s pathological retraction ring forms at this junction due to thinning of the lower uterine segment.

stem: You are performing an elective caesarean on a 28-year-old primigravida with a history of previous myomectomy breeching the uterine cavity. She has been appropriately consented and is keen to have more children. After delivery of the baby and intravenous administration of Oxytocin, you attempt to deliver the placenta but it is firmly adherent. The uterus is well contracted and is not bleeding significantly. What is the most appropriate management in this scenario?
option_C: Closure of the uterus and hysterectomy
gpt5_rationale: A firmly adherent placenta with a well-contracted, minimally bleeding uterus suggests placenta accreta; the appropriate management is to avoid manual removal and leave the placenta in situ with uterine closure to preserve fertility. None of the options offered reflects this conservative approach. Terbutaline and manual removal are inappropriate, hysterectomy is not indicated given stability and fertility wishes, and embolization is not the immediate step in this scenario.

stem: An 80-year-old develops steady, progressive memory and cognitive deficit over 2 years. He has normal blood pressure and no focal neurologic findings, and workup for “treatable” causes of dementia is negative. What is the likely diagnosis?
option_A: Senile dementia of the Alzheimer’s type
gpt5_rationale: Steady, progressive cognitive decline without focal neurologic signs and a negative workup for reversible causes is most consistent with Alzheimer’s disease. Multi-infarct dementia typically shows stepwise decline and focal deficits, and Lewy body dementia features fluctuating cognition and visual hallucinations.

stem: A 32-year-old female is seen at the hospital a 6-month history of worsening lethargy and weight gain over the past six months. She has no known medical conditions and works as a librarian. There is no anterior neck swelling. Her blood pressure is 110/80 mmHg, and her pulse is 67 bpm and regular. Her body mass index is 32 kg/m2. Thyroid function tests showed a normal TSH of 1.5 m IU/L but low free T 4 of 7.8 pmol/L . Which of the following is most likely for this patient.
gpt5_rationale: Low free T4 with an inappropriately normal TSH suggests central (secondary) hypothyroidism, not any of the listed primary thyroid disorders. The options provided (subacute thyroiditis, non-toxic goiter, Hashimoto’s, Graves’) do not match this biochemical pattern or presentation.

stem: A 72-year-old woman is found unconscious at home by her daughter. In the emergency room the patient does not respond to verbal or noxious stimuli. Which of the following is the most likely cause of her condition?
option_C: Lacunar infarct in the right internal capsule
gpt5_rationale: Coma without focal deficits is most commonly due to metabolic causes; hypoglycemia is a classic, reversible cause of unresponsiveness. The listed stroke localizations typically produce focal neurologic deficits rather than deep coma.

stem: A 27-year-old man presents to the hospital after falling from a tractor. There isa 12-cm wound over his thigh, and he has extensive soft tissue damage. The proximal femur is protruding from the wound. It appears that adequate soft tissue remains to allow for coverage of the wound. This is best classified as which type of open fracture
gpt5_rationale: This presentation is most consistent with a Gustilo Type IIIa open fracture: large wound with extensive soft tissue damage but adequate tissue for coverage. IIIb requires inadequate coverage needing flap, and IIIc requires arterial repair. IIIa is not listed among the options.

stem: A 15 year old boy with fever, altered sensorium and a purpuric rash for two days. He has extensive palpable purpura over the legs. Which of the following would be the most appropriate initial choice of antibiotic?
gpt5_rationale: This presentation suggests meningococcemia/meningitis with purpura. Among the options, chloramphenicol has good CNS penetration and activity against Neisseria meningitidis; vancomycin, ciprofloxacin, and gentamicin are not appropriate monotherapies here.

stem: A 17-year-old, pregnant woman was brought to maternity ward, because was found to have generalized convulsion at the central market. O/E: (positive finding) unconscious, pale +, BP 156/100 mmHg, hyperreflexia, urine dipstick for protein +; F/L 35 cm. V/E Cervix effaced, dilated 4 cm, station –1. How do you manage this patient?
option_A: General measure, prophylactic antibiotic and immediate C/section.
option_B: General measures, antihypertensive, MgSO4, resuscitation of the patient, foetal assessment and emergency c/section.
option_C: General measures, antihypertensive, anticonvulsant and augmentation.
option_D: General measures, BP control, fit control, mother stabilization and conservative management.
gpt5_rationale: This is eclampsia in active labor (4 cm, effaced). Stabilize with general measures, control BP, give anticonvulsant (MgSO4), and expedite vaginal delivery with augmentation if feasible; emergency cesarean is reserved for obstetric indications or failed progress.

stem: A 30-year-old woman with bipolar disorder on lithium therapy presents with polyuria and polydipsia. Random blood sugar checked was 6.8 mmol/L . Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
gpt5_rationale: Lithium commonly causes nephrogenic diabetes insipidus due to chronic tubulointerstitial nephropathy, presenting with polyuria and polydipsia. The random glucose is not in the diabetic range, making diabetes mellitus unlikely, and membranous GN or psychogenic polydipsia are less consistent with this context.

stem: A 70-year-old male complains of the sudden onset of syncope. It occurs without warning and with no sweating, dizziness, or lightheadedness. He believes episodes tend to occur when he turns his head too quickly or sometimes when he is shaving. The best way to make a definitive diagnosis in this patient is which of the following?
option_D: Electrophysiologic studies to evaluate the AV node
gpt5_rationale: Syncope precipitated by head turning or shaving in an older man suggests carotid sinus hypersensitivity. The definitive test is carotid sinus massage with ECG (and BP) monitoring to reproduce symptoms and document bradycardia or hypotension.

stem: A 20 years old para 0+0 at 38 weeks gestation with a B. P 160/110 multg, severe headache and one episode of convulsion one hour ago. Obsteric examination revealed a term fundal height, cephalic presentation and fetal heart rate of 144/min. The cervix is closed 2 cm long, posterior and not effaced. What would be your next plan of action?
option_A: Start magnesium sulphate intravenous hydralazine and prepare for emergency caesarean section delivery
option_B: Start magnesium sulphate then control blood pressure with I.V hydrallazine and insert postraglandin pessary.
option_C: Start intravenous valium, hydrallazine drip and prepare for emergency caesarean section delivery.
option_D: Start intravenous labetalel drip and prepare for emergency ceasarean section delivery
gpt5_rationale: This is eclampsia at term with severe hypertension and recent seizure. Immediate stabilization with magnesium sulfate and IV antihypertensive is indicated, followed by expedited delivery; with an unfavorable cervix, emergency cesarean section is appropriate. Options without magnesium or proposing induction are not suitable in this acute setting.

stem: A 50-year-old woman is seen in clinic because of weight loss, restlessness, and palpitations. She also has noted leg swelling and excessive hair loss. Her past medical history is unremarkable. She takes no medications. She is afebrile. On exam, she is tachycardic and has a fine tremor. She has mild exophthalmos. Her thyroid is smooth and uniformly enlarged. TSH levels are low and T 3 and T 4 levels are elevated. What is the next best step in management?
gpt5_rationale: Findings are consistent with Graves disease. The immediate next step is to control adrenergic symptoms with a beta-blocker such as propranolol; definitive therapy (antithyroid drugs, radioactive iodine, or surgery) is considered afterward, and radioactive iodine may worsen ophthalmopathy.

stem:
2)	A 4-year-old girl is brought to the emergency department by her aunt because the child
has suddenly become clumsy, and her speech has become slurred over the last hour. On
physical examination, the girl is afebrile and dysarthric. She has prominent vertical and
horizontal nystagmus, along with truncal and appendicular ataxia. Deep tendon reflexes are
normal, as are results of the remainder of the physical examination.
Of the following, the MOST likely diagnosis is
gpt5_rationale: Sudden-onset dysarthria, bidirectional nystagmus, and truncal/appendicular ataxia point to an acute cerebellar process, most consistent with hemorrhage. Lack of fever or meningismus argues against meningoencephalitis; brain tumors present more insidiously; Guillain-Barré causes areflexic weakness, not cerebellar signs.

stem: 94. A 50-year-old presents to the emergency room with a sudden onset abdominal pain an hour ago. He is hypotensive with a board-like rigidity of the abdomen. The presence of guarding in the abdomen is suggestive of what type of physiologic pain
gpt5_rationale: Guarding and board-like rigidity indicate peritoneal irritation, which is characteristic of parietal (somatic) pain. Parietal pain is sharp and localized, whereas visceral pain is dull and poorly localized.

stem: A 48-year-old woman presents with a non-tender, hard mass in the right submandibular triangle (Level I), which has enlarged slowly over the past year. The mass is 2.5 cm in diameter, firm, and feels like it is in the submandibular gland. Saliva can be expressed from the right Wharton's duct. This mass:
option_A: Is likely an inflammatory node in the submandibular triangle.
option_B: Is likely a malignant tumor of the submandibular gland
option_C: a benign tumor of the submandibular gland.
gpt5_rationale: A slow-growing, firm, non-tender mass within the submandibular gland with patent duct flow is most consistent with a benign pleomorphic adenoma. Inflammatory nodes are less likely, and malignant tumors more often show rapid growth, pain, or infiltrative signs.

stem:
A 34-year-old male patient presents in casualty with right lower abdominal pain. The patient is evaluated and a CT scan urogram confirms a 2 mm calculi in the right ureter at vesico-ureteric junction. Patient is referred to a urologist for advice. All of the following are true advice 
EXCEPT:
option_A: Patient is advised to maintain good hydration
option_B: Patient can be managed conservatively as he is being followed up in the clinic
option_C: Renal function test are important while on follow-up
gpt5_rationale: For a 2 mm distal ureteric stone, advising hydration, conservative management with follow-up, monitoring renal function, and prioritizing pain control are all appropriate. None of the options is clearly incorrect.

stem: A 29-year-old man is reviewed in the General Neurology Clinic. He has experienced progressive deterioration in his gait over the past 11 years, such that he currently needs the assistance of a frame. Over the last 2 years, his hearing has progressively worsened. A detailed family history reveals that his maternal grandmother had epilepsy, his mother experienced progressive deafness in middle age and his mother’s sister had difficulty walking and was told she had multiple sclerosis. Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis in this case?
gpt5_rationale: Progressive gait impairment with sensorineural hearing loss and a maternal lineage of diverse neurologic issues strongly suggests a maternally inherited mitochondrial disorder. Friedreich’s ataxia is autosomal recessive and typically involves cardiomyopathy; Huntington’s disease presents with chorea and cognitive decline; Miller–Fisher syndrome is acute/subacute, not progressive over years.

stem: A 38-year-old had not passed urine after open haemorrhoidectomy. He admitted to having severe pain at the surgical site which made him quite apprehensive. What is the first intervention that should be started in the management of this acute urinary retention
option_C: c.	To do a formal suprapubic stab cystostomy
gpt5_rationale: Post-hemorrhoidectomy urinary retention is commonly pain-induced; first-line management is adequate analgesia and reassurance to facilitate voiding. Catheterization is considered if retention persists despite pain control.

stem: A psychiatric Senior House Officer is called to consult on the case of a 75-year-old woman who had undergone a hip replacement two days before. On examination, the House Officer notes that the patient states the date as 1956, and she thinks she is at her son’s house. These impairments illustrate which aspect of the mental status examination?
gpt5_rationale: Stating the wrong date and location reflects disorientation to time and place, which is part of the orientation component of the mental status exam. This is not primarily a memory, concentration, or thought process issue.

stem: A 48-year-old diabetic man, who has already undergone an amputation of his left foot from gangrene last year, presents with three days history of bloody diarrhoea. What would be the clinical symptoms he would exhibit that would suggest he has a vascular cause of his GI bleeding?
option_A: Chronic history of recurring bouts of bloody diarrhoea with pain and occasional jaundice
option_B: Presence of claudication symptoms in the lower extremities
option_C: Recent travel history with consumption of lake water as diabetics are more susceptible to water-borne infections
option_D: Early satiety with weight loss but without abdominal pain
gpt5_rationale: Claudication indicates peripheral arterial disease and systemic atherosclerosis, which strongly predispose to intestinal ischemia (e.g., ischemic colitis) presenting with bloody diarrhea. The other options suggest infectious or neoplastic causes rather than a vascular etiology.

stem: A two-year old child presents at the emergency unit with a 2-day history of fever, cough and sialorrhoea. Examination shows enlarged jugulodigastric lymph nodes. Which of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
gpt5_rationale: Jugulodigastric (tonsillar) lymph node enlargement and sialorrhoea suggest tonsillar/pharyngeal inflammation. Fever and cough can accompany upper airway infections. Pertussis and coryza are less consistent with drooling, and pneumonia would typically have lower respiratory signs.

stem: 26)	A primiparous woman whose blood type is O positive gives birth at term to an infant who has A-positive blood and a hematocrit of 55%. A total serum bilirubin level obtained at 36 hours of age is 12 mg/dL . Which of the following additional laboratory findings would be characteristic of ABO hemolytic disease in this infant?
option_D: Crescent-shaped red blood cells in the blood smear
gpt5_rationale: ABO hemolytic disease typically shows hemolysis with elevated reticulocytes and sometimes spherocytes; hemoglobin is not elevated, petechiae are unrelated, a normal reticulocyte count is incorrect, and sickled cells are not seen. None of the choices matches the characteristic lab findings.

stem: A 23-year old woman undergoes splenectomy following trauma. On postoperative day four, she developstachycardia and peritonitis, andshe has dark, bloody output from the nasogastric tube. What is the complication of greatest concern?
gpt5_rationale: After splenectomy, ligation of short gastric vessels can cause fundal gastric ischemia leading to necrosis and perforation, presenting with peritonitis and dark, bloody NG output. Varices are unlikely in a young trauma patient, OPSI typically presents as sepsis without upper GI bleeding this early, and a missed enterotomy would not explain the bloody NG output.

stem: CURB-65 criteria include five variables-confusion (C), urea >7 mmol/L (U), respiratory rate 30/ minute (R), blood pressure, systolic 90 mmHg or diastolic 60 mmHg (B) and age 65 years (65). Patients should be admitted to the hospital when the CURB-65 score is more than?
gpt5_rationale: CURB-65 scores 0–1 are generally treated as outpatient; ≥2 warrants hospital admission/observation. Therefore, admission is recommended when the score is more than 1.

stem: The obstetric doctor on call is asked to review a cardiotocograph performed prior to induction of labour in a 39/40 multigravida. Which of the following features should help to reassure the doctor that this is a normal trace?
option_A: The baseline variability is <5 beats per minute.
option_B: There are no significant accelerations of the fetal heart on the 30-minute recording.
option_C: The tocograph is picking up regular contractions.
option_D: The baseline heart rate is 100 beats per minute.
gpt5_rationale: Normal CTG features include baseline 110–160 bpm and variability 5–25 bpm with accelerations as a reassuring sign. A shows reduced variability, B shows absence of a reassuring feature, C reflects uterine activity not fetal well-being, and D indicates bradycardia (<110 bpm). None are reassuring.

stem: A 30-year-old woman presents to the early pregnancy unit with mild vaginal bleeding. An ultrasound is performed, which reports a complete molar pregnancy. What would be the parental origin and genetic complement of the complete molar pregnancy?
gpt5_rationale: A complete mole is androgenetic diploid with two paternal sets and no maternal genome. None of the options correctly state ‘2 paternal sets: diploid.’

stem: A 70-year-old male with history of hypertension and diabetes presents with a stepwise loss of intellectual function. Prior episodes have been associated with unilateral weakness and difficulty swallowing. A unilateral Babinski sign is found on neurological exam. What is the likely diagnosis?
option_A: Senile dementia of the Alzheimer’s type
gpt5_rationale: Stepwise cognitive decline with focal neurological deficits (unilateral weakness, dysphagia) and a unilateral Babinski sign in a patient with vascular risk factors is characteristic of multi-infarct (vascular) dementia. Alzheimer’s, B12 deficiency, and Lewy body dementia typically do not present with a stepwise course and focal findings.

stem: . A 60-year-old man complains of a lump in front of the ear lobe. The lump has been growing gradually for years, and there is now a visiblemass. He does not have any pain or facial weakness. His examinationis otherwise normal. What is the most likely diagnosis?
gpt5_rationale: A slow-growing, painless parotid mass over years without facial nerve involvement is classic for a benign parotid tumor, most commonly a pleomorphic adenoma. Malignant salivary tumors are more likely to present with pain, rapid growth, or facial nerve weakness.

stem: You are asked to see a 35-year old female who sustained a head injury during a road traffic accident. The patient was stable upon arrival to the emergency department but over the last hour his GCS has fallen from 15/15 to 13/15 and is now is drowsy and complaining of a headache. What is the most appropriate next step to take in the patient’s management?
option_A: Repeat the GCS score in another 3 hours
option_B: Speak with the neurosurgeon on call with a view of taking the patient to theatre
